Attentional lock; difficulty disengaging from cued location; hypervigilant orienting
Cancel all circles on mixed-shape sheet. Speed and accuracy.
Slow; >120 sec; omissions; poor inhibitory drive
Fast with false alarms; impulsive cancelling; attentional hyperactivation
Letter cancellation 5 min; compare first vs last 2.5 min hit rate.
>30% decline; rapid fatigue; poor sustained drive
No decline but accelerating errors; hypervigilant pace maintained at cost of accuracy

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.title: Medication & Clinical Notes
network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.

rows: Tell a simple false belief story: 'Sally puts her ball in a basket. Anne moves it. Where will Sally look?' Note correct mentalizing.
Correct: 'In the basket'; mentalizing intact; DMN social circuit preserved
Gives random answer; global cognitive impairment; or very young
Correct answer but excessive processing time or requires repeated explanation; ASD mentalizing delay
10 adjectives with self-reference frame. Recall after 2-min distractor.
<4/10; reduced self-reference effect; ASD self-schema HYPO; alexithymia
Normal recall but only non-social self-descriptors (systematic, rule-following) recalled
Ask 3 personal memories. Note: are memories semantic/fact-based or episodically rich with emotional content?
Episodically rich; balanced social and nonsocial content
Sparse; very brief; poor emotional autobiographical content; ASD pattern
Highly detailed factual memories but lacking social-emotional context; ASD memory style
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note: is off-task thinking related to restricted interests?
3-4/4 off-task; content specifically restricted interest topics; ASD DMN special interest
Ask: 'What might the examiner be thinking right now?' Rate ToM response quality.
Generates plausible examiner perspective; mentalization intact
'I don't know'; refuses social perspective; ASD mentalizing HYPO
Generates literal/factual guess only; no social inference; ASD perspective-taking deficit
Recalls exactly at 5 min or recites rule repeatedly; ASD rule-following recall
Ask: 'Tell me about a social situation from this week.' Rate social content and emotional integration.
Coherent social narrative; emotional perspective-taking present
Minimal social content; prefers nonsocial topics; avoids social narrative
Factual social narrative without emotional integration; ASD social-DMN pattern
Span ≥5; may be higher in ASD (strong rote memory)
Span ≤3; intellectual disability overlap; global cognitive HYPO
Span ≥6; ASD enhanced rote working memory; preserved to superior
Slow >150 sec; ASD set-shifting difficulty; executive HYPO
Fast with good accuracy; ASD rule-following strength; executive pattern strength
Letter fluency 60 sec per letter. Note: does ASD systematize output?
<6; poor verbal fluency; language delay overlap
≥12 words but from restricted semantic field; ASD systematic fluency pattern
Name ink color. Note: does ASD rule-following paradoxically reduce Stroop interference?
Slow; impulsive errors; poor rule maintenance
Fast; minimal interference; ASD rigid rule-following reduces Stroop effect
5/5 rapidly; ASD systematic arithmetic strength; enhanced CEN for rule-based tasks
Tap for all except X. 20 letters.
High omissions; poor task engagement; ASD social-task disengagement
Very low errors; rigid rule-following; ASD rule-adherence
Alternating fruits and vegetables for 60 sec.
<5 alternations; cognitive inflexibility; ASD category rigidity
Normal count but insists on predictable alternating rule; ASD systematizing
Show 5 images: 3 with social scenes, 2 nonsocial. Ask what patient noticed first in each.
Social elements noticed first in social images; appropriate orienting
No social orienting; focuses exclusively on objects or background; ASD SN social HYPO
Social elements noticed but without emotional significance attributed; ASD social-SN HYPO
Rate sensory discomfort in 5 modalities (sound, light, touch, smell, taste) 0-10 each.
Average ≤2; sensory hyposensitivity; ASD sensory HYPO subtype
≥7 in ≥2 modalities; sensory hypersensitivity; ASD SN HYPER sensory subtype
Low accuracy <0.50; ASD interoceptive awareness HYPO; alexithymia
High accuracy; strong interoceptive awareness; ASD interoceptive HYPER subtype
Note reaction to making an error during assessment. Rate distress vs. neutral response.
Mild appropriate response; tolerates error; moves on
No response; does not register social significance of error; ASD SN HYPO
Extreme distress; rigid perfectionism; cannot move on; ASD OCD overlap
Apply light fingertip touch to back of hand with cotton. Rate: aversiveness 0-10.
0; no tactile response; hyposensitivity; ASD tactile HYPO
≥7; tactile aversiveness; tactile defensiveness; ASD sensory SN HYPER
Alternate visual and auditory tasks. Note sensory channel preference and switching.
Cannot switch from preferred sensory modality; ASD sensory perseveration
Switches correctly but with visible distress at modality change; ASD sensory inflexibility
Rate 6 emotional (3 social, 3 nonsocial interesting) and 6 neutral images 0-10.
Balanced; social emotional images rated appropriately salient
Flat; no differential salience; ASD emotional-social blunting
Nonsocial/interesting images rated far higher than social emotional; ASD salience inversion
Examiner demonstrates 3 novel hand gestures. Ask patient to imitate immediately.
Accurate imitation of ≥2/3 gestures; automatic mirroring
Refuses or unable to imitate; global motor impairment
Accurate but delayed; requires explicit instruction; ASD imitation delay (not loss)
Observe throughout assessment for repetitive motor behaviors (hand-flapping, rocking, finger-rubbing, pacing).
0-1 stereotyped movements per 10 min; socially modulated
0; no stereotypy; ASD without prominent motor stereotypy; or masking
≥3 stereotyped movement types; increased with emotional arousal; ASD SMN HYPER
Normal rate but unusual rhythm pattern; ASD motor variability
Normal but excessively precise; ASD systematic motor execution
Multiple failures; ASD-DCD motor coordination deficit
Normal with excessively rigid posture; ASD motor rigidity
Normal grip but unusual grip style; excessively stiff; ASD motor variability
Regular but with unusual motor quality; ASD motor singularity
Observe natural gait for 5 steps. Note: toe-walking, arm-swing asymmetry, unusual body posture.
Symmetric; heel-toe; bilateral arm swing; normal posture
Normal gait but reduced spontaneity; low motor engagement
Toe-walking; reduced arm swing; unusual posture; ASD-specific gait markers
Identify 6 emotions from facial photos. Score /6. Note fixation on mouth vs eyes.
≥5/6 correct; uses eye region for recognition
<4/6; ASD facial affect recognition failure; limbic-social HYPO
4/6; uses mouth or non-diagnostic features; ASD non-typical recognition strategy
Ask: 'When you feel something, can you describe what that feeling is?' Rate 0-10.
≥6; can label emotions with some description; emotional awareness maintained
0-3; poor emotional awareness; alexithymia; ASD limbic HYPO
Extreme difficulty labeling own emotions despite preserved emotion recognition
Flat; 'nothing'; limited emotional vocabulary; ASD alexithymia
Correct but over-literal; misses emotional nuance; ASD concrete limbic processing
Ask: 'How do you think that person felt in this story?' Rate perspective-taking quality.
Generates plausible emotional perspective; empathic reasoning
Cannot generate emotional perspective; ASD cognitive empathy HYPO
Generates factual description only; 'they would be cold' not 'frightened'; ASD empathy
Reward Sensitivity — Special Interest vs Social
Rate interest in 5 activities: 3 related to possible special interest, 2 social activities.
Balanced interest across types; ≥5/10 for social activities
Low interest in all; global anhedonia; ASD-depression overlap
10/10 for special interest; 0-2 for social; ASD reward circuit asymmetry
Ask: 'When overwhelmed, what do you do?' Rate emotional regulation strategies 0-10.
Describes ≥2 coping strategies; emotion regulation maintained
Describes avoidance as primary strategy; limited regulation repertoire
Reports meltdowns; overwhelm; inability to regulate; ASD limbic HYPER in overload
Sad then happy memory. Rate mood shift.
Flat; no shift; ASD emotional blunting; alexithymia pattern
Mood shift but extreme difficulty initiating; ASD emotional inertia
Point to object across room. Note: does patient follow gaze/point? Rate joint attention 0-10.
Follows point; establishes joint attention; social orienting maintained
Does not follow; reduced joint attention; ASD VAN social HYPO
Follows but does not share attention socially; looks at object only; ASD joint attention
Ask: 'Do you ever become so absorbed in an activity you lose track of time?' Rate hyperfocus frequency 0-10.
0; no hyperfocus; inattentive ASD subtype; or flat engagement
≥8; hyperfocus; cannot disengage; ASD attention HYPER for special interests
Circle all A's in 20-item letter list.
Slow; multiple omissions; ASD attention engagement deficit
Fast; very accurate; ASD detail-focused attention strength
Performs both but excessively slow; ASD monotropic attention pattern
Fails; does not engage with search task
<10 sec; faster than neurotypical; ASD superior visual search (embedded figures strength)
Absent alerting; poor preparatory readiness; ASD social-task disengagement
Over-alerting; rigid anticipatory state; ASD anxiety-attention overlap
Observe eye contact during 5-min assessment. Rate ASD-specific social attention pattern.
Natural variable eye contact; appropriate social attention
Absent eye contact; severe social attention HYPO; classic ASD marker
Prolonged staring; atypical social attention pattern; ASD social attention anomaly
Switch from letter cancellation to symbol copying mid-task without warning. Measure switch cost.
Adapts within 5 sec; switch cost <30 sec
Cannot switch; rigid; requires extensive instruction; ASD inflexibility HYPO
Switches but with extreme distress at transition; ASD cognitive inflexibility

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.title: Medication & Clinical Notes
network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.

rows: Ask: 'When pain is worst, do thoughts about it repeat involuntarily?' Rate duration and controllability 0-10.
Minimal rumination despite high pain; dissociation from pain
Uncontrollable pain thoughts ≥8/10 for >2 hours; chronic pain DMN hallmark
Ask patient to narrate pain history: onset, evolution, current impact.
Coherent; some positive reframing; functional goals mentioned
Sparse; denies pain impact; stoic suppression
Entirely suffering-focused; hopeless; no positive elements; catastrophizing
Ask: 'Would you say chronic pain is part of who you are?' Rate 0-10.
0-4; pain acknowledged but not identity-defining; adaptive coping
≥8; pain as complete identity; loss of non-pain self-concept
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note: when off-task, is content pain-related?
3-4/4 off-task; pain-specific thought content; pain-DMN capture
Ask 3 questions: 'Will pain never get better? Do you feel helpless? Do you magnify pain?' Rate 0-10 each.
All ≤3; realistic pain appraisal; self-efficacy maintained
≥7 on ≥2 items; high catastrophizing; pain DMN hyperactivation
Forgets; cognitive load from pain impairs prospective memory
Repeatedly reminds before time; pain-driven hypermonitoring
Ask: 'What activities have you given up because of pain?' Rate acceptance vs avoidance framing.
Acknowledges losses but maintains goals; ACT-compatible
Denies giving up activities; minimization; avoidant
Extensive list; nothing maintained; complete pain-driven life restriction
Span ≤3; cognitive dysfunction from chronic pain
60 sec per letter. Count unique words.
6-9; mild reduction; attentional drain from pain
Include pain-related words (hurt, ache, sharp). Compare interference.
No Stroop effect; dissociation from pain
Selectively slowed on pain words; pain-emotional Stroop effect
Offer 2 task options. Note if pain appraisal impairs decision-making.
Cannot decide; helplessness; pain-driven executive failure
Rapid pain-avoidant decisions; pain drives all choices
Normal count; slow pacing; effortful due to pain
Ask patient to indicate pain locations on body. Count regions affected.
≥7 widespread regions; fibromyalgia pattern; central sensitization; SN HYPER
Apply light cotton touch to forearm. Ask if painful.
Light touch not painful; no allodynia; normal threshold
No pain to any stimulus; sensory blunting; hypoalgesia; HYPO
Light touch reported as painful; allodynia; central sensitization; SN HYPER
Count heartbeats 25 sec. Ask: 'Are you aware of pain during concentration?'
Accuracy 0.75; pain remains in background during concentration
Low accuracy; poor body awareness; dissociation
High accuracy AND heightened pain awareness during focus; hyperinteroception
Misses both; cognitive load from pain
Catches errors; uses for self-criticism; pain-mood interaction
Apply thumb pressure to trapezius and calf bilaterally. Rate pain 0-10 each.
≤4 at all sites; no widespread tenderness
0 at all sites; hypoalgesia; analgesia; HYPO
≥6 at ≥3 sites; widespread tenderness; fibromyalgia SN HYPER
Alternate visual and auditory tasks. Note if switching triggers pain increase.
Poor switching; cognitive inertia; pain-cognitive rigidity
Switching triggers pain or headache; sensory overload; fibromyalgia SN HYPER
Unexpected auditory tone. Note if tone triggers pain response.
Brief interruption; reorientation <3 sec; no pain increase
Exaggerated startle; tone triggers pain; central sensitization of startle-pain circuit
Index tapping 10 sec each hand. Note pain with movement.
Normal rate; patient reports pain increase during tapping; movement hyperalgesia
Bilateral grip. Note pain with gripping.
Firm symmetric; no pain with gripping
Normal grip but pain ≥7 during gripping; movement-induced pain amplification
3-4 sequences; pain awareness heightened during fine motor
Rise from chair without arm support; turn; sit back. Time the sequence.
Cannot perform; severe pain-inhibited movement; kinesiophobia
Performs but >20 sec; reports pain ≥7; fear-avoidance
Stable but excessive tensing; guarding; protective motor pattern
Rate pressure at 4 tender points (bilateral trapezius, bilateral knee) 0-10.
0-2 at each site; no diffuse tenderness
0 pain at all sites; hypoalgesia; analgesia; HYPO
≥6 at ≥3 sites; fibromyalgia diagnosis support; SN HYPER
Ask: 'Do you stop before pain starts (pacing) or push until pain stops you?'
Paces; activity matched to capacity; adaptive
Denies any pain limitation; avoidant minimization
Boom-bust pattern; over-does then crashes; maladaptive pacing; pain chronification
Hand lift when pen drops. 3 trials average.
<300 ms; pain does not delay initiation
<250 ms but pain increases after fast movement; reactive hyperalgesia
Ask: 'Are you afraid of what your pain means for your future?' Rate 0-10.
≥8; catastrophic pain fear; kinesiophobia; limbic HYPER core feature
Flat; 'nothing'; pain has numbed emotional response
All emotions flavored by pain-frustration; labels anxiety across all scenarios
10 words (5 pain/negative, 5 positive). Recall after 5-min distractor.
Flat recall; pain cognitive load impairs emotional processing
Pain/negative words preferentially recalled; positive suppressed; pain-mood bias
Rate interest in 5 enjoyable activities 0-10. Note: avoidance from pain fear vs anhedonia.
Average ≤3; pain-driven anhedonia; avoidance from fear
High interest but pain prevents participation; preserved motivation with pain barrier
Rate comfort in 5 social scenarios 0-10. Note pain as barrier.
≤3; avoidance from pain in social contexts; shame; isolation
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no shift; pain-induced emotional blunting
Sad memory activates pain; pain prevents positive affect; limbic-pain loop
Count backward from 50. At pain moment: how does performance change? Rate interference 0-10.
Maintains counting with mild pain; ≤2 errors
Doesn't stop despite severe pain; dissociation possible
Completely stops at pain flare; pain captures all attentional resources
Slow; multiple omissions; cognitive interference from pain
Normal but interspersed with pain rubs; pain breaks attentional continuity
Abandons one; pain absorbs cognitive resources
Completes but reports pain increase with dual-task; cognitive-pain interaction
20-40 sec; systematic but interrupted by pain attention
Observe attention to body during assessment. Count body-directed behaviors (touching painful area, wincing).
≤2 body-directed behaviors per 5 min; balanced attention
≥5 body-directed behaviors; body hypervigilance; VAN HYPER for pain cues
Unexpected tone. Note if it triggers body awareness increase.
Brief interruption; no pain increase; reorientation <3 sec
Tone triggers body scan; pain awareness increase; SN-VAN hyperactivation
Normal time but interrupted by pain breaks; continuity disruption

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.title: Medication & Clinical Notes
network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.

rows: Ask: 'When feeling sad, do thoughts repeat involuntarily for >5 min?' Rate frequency 0–10 and duration
Minimal intrusive thoughts; able to redirect within 2 min
Blunted self-reflection; empty thought content; emotional numbness
Persistent negative intrusive thoughts >5 min; unable to redirect; ruminative loops
Ask for 3 specific personal memories (childhood, recent, positive event). Rate detail, accuracy, and emotional tone
Vivid, specific memories with mixed emotional content; appropriate affect
Sparse memory detail; no associated emotion; apathetic recall
Predominantly negative memory retrieval; catastrophic reinterpretation of neutral events
Read 10 adjectives with 'Does this describe you?' frame. Free recall after 2 min distractor. Count self-referenced words.
≥6/10 recalled; balanced positive and negative self-descriptors
<4/10 recalled; emotional flatness; difficulty with self-referential processing
All negative self-descriptors endorsed and recalled; positive words rejected or not recalled
Give 2-min reading passage; interrupt 4 times randomly: 'Were you thinking about the text?' Note task-unrelated thought rate
0/4 off-task — blunted ideation; emotionally flat or dissociated
3–4/4 off-task; reports persistent sad/worry thoughts during reading
Tell patient: 'Remind me to ask about your sleep in 5 minutes.' Continue assessment. Note spontaneous recall.
Forgets entirely; no self-monitoring; poor future orientation
Perseveratively asks before 5 min; intrusive future-oriented worry
Ask: 'Tell me something about yourself from the past week.' Rate: coherence, self-reference, negative bias, time-orientation
Balanced narrative; positive/neutral events described; normal temporal orientation
Minimal narrative content; flat affect; difficulty generating self-relevant material
Exclusively negative content; rumination on failures/losses; catastrophic framing
Name personal possessions or meaningful places in 1 min. Count items; note emotional valence of examples
≥8 items; mixed emotional valence; no perseveration
<4 items; flat responses; poor self-schema access
Predominantly negative/loss-associated items; perseveration on depressive themes
Read digit sequences; ask reverse recall. Start 2-digits, increase by 1 each trial. Score max length achieved.
Backward span ≤3; poor working memory; rapid forgetting
Span intact but slow; perseverative responses; minor executive intrusions
Draw alternating number-letter connections (1-A-2-B-3-C...) on prepared sheet. Record time and errors.
Extremely slow >180 sec; multiple errors; gives up mid-task
Impulsive sequence errors; rushes without accuracy; mild planning deficits
Name words beginning with F in 60 sec. Repeat for A and S. Count unique words; note repetitions.
<6 words; flat verbal output; poor initiating
Normal count but prolonged pauses; intrusions of depressive content
Show color-word cards. Name ink color only, ignoring printed word. Record time and errors for 20 items.
Extremely slow; disengaged; requires prompting to continue
Moderate interference errors; emotional Stroop effect on negative words
Count backward from 100 by 7s. Record 5 subtractions. Note time, errors, and patient effort/initiation.
≤2/5 correct; poor initiation; requires encouragement; effortful
4–5/5 correct but slow; perseverative self-criticism on errors
Read 20 letters at 1/sec; patient taps for every letter except X. Count commission errors (tapped X) and omission errors.
High omission errors (>5); poor inhibitory initiation; psychomotor slowing
Moderate commission errors; impulsive responding despite depressed affect
Name alternating fruits then vegetables (apple, broccoli, pear...) for 60 sec. Count switches and intrusion errors.
<5 alternations; difficulty switching; flat verbal output
Normal switching but slowed initiation; mild set-maintenance errors
Count own heartbeats mentally for 25 sec without touching body. Compare to actual count. Calculate interoceptive accuracy score.
Low accuracy <0.50; poor body awareness; alexithymia pattern
Overestimation of heartbeats; hyperawareness of somatic sensations; health anxiety
Read sentence with 2 embedded factual errors aloud. Note whether patient spontaneously self-corrects or identifies errors.
Misses both errors; poor self-monitoring; apathetic processing
Extreme distress on noticing own errors; self-critical rumination; catastrophizing
Show 6 emotional (3 negative, 3 positive) and 6 neutral images. Rate personal salience/importance 0–10.
Balanced salience across valences; negatives ≤7, positives ≥4
Flat salience ratings across all images; anhedonic; emotional blunting
Negative images rated ≥9; positive images rated ≤2; extreme negativity bias
Present 3 ambiguous social scenarios in writing. Rate perceived threat 0–10.
Threat ratings 2–5 for ambiguous scenarios; balanced interpretation
Threat ratings ≤2 across all scenarios including objectively negative ones; emotional blunting
All scenarios rated ≥7; catastrophic interpretation of neutral social events
Alternate between visual tracking (follow moving pen) and auditory counting (count tones). Measure switch latency.
Slow switching >5 sec; poor disengagement; psychomotor inertia
Impulsive switching; insufficient task maintenance; distractibility
Apply firm thumb pressure to fingernail bed for 3 sec. Rate intensity 0–10. Compare bilateral.
Rating 3–6; bilateral symmetry; appropriate facial response
Low pain ratings ≤2; emotional blunting; reduced somatic awareness
Rating ≥8; hyperalgesia; somatic amplification consistent with depression-pain comorbidity
Deliver unexpected auditory tone (clap) mid-task. Observe behavioral interruption, reorientation time, and recovery.
Minimal orienting; blunted startle; disengaged vigilance
Prolonged behavioral disruption; exaggerated distress; slow recovery
Index finger tap on table as fast as possible for 10 sec. Count taps. Test dominant then non-dominant hand.
<35 taps; psychomotor retardation; slowed movement initiation
≥60 taps but with agitation; restless tapping; psychomotor agitation subtype
Ask patient to squeeze examiner's two fingers as firmly as possible. Compare left vs right. Rate bilateral symmetry.
Weak bilateral grip; slow force generation; reduced motor drive
Normal grip but perseverative squeezing; agitated, can't release
Thumb-to-finger opposition sequence (1-2-3-4-4-3-2-1) at maximum speed. Count complete sequences in 10 sec.
≤2 sequences; sequential errors; reduced motor speed and fluency
3–4 sequences but with agitated interruptions; motor restlessness
Walk heel-to-toe on a straight line for 10 steps. Count steps off line. Observe posture and gait pattern.
≤1 step off line; upright posture; normal pace
Slow pace; stooped posture; multiple step failures; psychomotor slowing
Restless gait; unable to slow to tandem; agitated movement
Stand feet together, arms crossed, eyes closed for 30 sec. Observe postural sway, balance, and stance.
Minimal sway; stable stance for 30 sec
Excessive sway; requires wall support; poor proprioceptive engagement
Fidgeting/shifting weight during standing; unable to remain still; agitation
Rapidly pronate and supinate forearm alternating for 10 sec on lap. Observe rhythm, speed, and bilateral coordination.
Slow; irregular rhythm; reduced motor amplitude; effortful movements
Irregular rhythm; agitated rapid but uncoordinated movements
Write name and address in cursive; draw 10 connected circles. Assess size consistency, pressure, and writing speed.
Legible; consistent letter size; moderate pressure; smooth circles
Micrographia; reduced writing speed; poor pressure; incomplete circles
Normal size but tremor/scrawl from agitation; irregular letter spacing
Lift dominant hand from table as fast as possible when examiner drops pen. Measure 3 trials and average.
Reaction time >450 ms; psychomotor slowing; delayed motor initiation
Anticipatory responses (lifts before drop); motor restlessness; impulsive
Show 6 photos representing: joy, sadness, fear, anger, disgust, surprise. Ask to identify each. Score /6.
<4/6; blunted affect recognition; emotional numbing pattern
Hypersensitivity to sad/fearful faces; misidentification of neutral as negative
Describe 3 emotional scenarios (loss of a loved one, receiving praise, unexpected noise). Ask: 'What emotion would you feel?'
Names appropriate distinct emotions for each scenario
Flat responses; 'I don't know' or 'nothing'; alexithymia; emotional blunting
Excessive negative emotion labeling; catastrophic responses to all scenarios
Read 10 emotional (5 negative, 5 positive) and 10 neutral words. Free recall after 5-min distractor task.
Equal or slight positive recall advantage; ≥8/20 total
Flat recall; poor emotional enhancement of memory; anhedonic
Selective negative word recall; positive words suppressed; depressive memory bias
Present 3 mildly threatening scenarios verbally. Rate anxiety 0–10. Observe physiological signs (breathing, posture).
Anxiety ratings 2–5; proportionate responses; quick recovery
Flat anxiety response ≤2 even to objectively threatening scenarios; blunted fear
Anxiety ≥8 to all scenarios; catastrophic fear response; breathing changes
Rate interest in 5 previously enjoyed activities (hobbies, food, social interaction) on 0–10 scale.
Average ≤3/10; pervasive anhedonia; loss of pleasure (core Depression marker)
Average ≥8 but with anxious over-engagement; manic-mixed features
Rate comfort in 5 social scenarios: crowded room, intimate conversation, meeting strangers, being observed, being alone.
Comfort ≥5/10 for ≥4 scenarios; social engagement maintained
All scenarios rated ≤3; social withdrawal; isolation; depressive avoidance
Anxious social discomfort ≥8 for all scenarios; social anxiety comorbidity
Recall a sad personal memory (rate mood). Then recall a happy memory (rate mood). Measure shift magnitude and recovery time.
Mood shift ≥3 points and return to baseline within 3 min
Flat mood across both memories; no emotional responsivity; anhedonic rigidity
Sad memory causes prolonged low mood >10 min; unable to access positive affect
Count backward from 50 aloud continuously. Rate performance consistency over 60 sec. Note fadeouts.
Counts consistently; ≤2 errors or stops in 60 sec
Frequent pauses; stops mid-count; poor sustained engagement; effortful
Monotonous perfect count with agitated pace; pressured speech subtype
In a list of 20 mixed letters, circle only the letter 'A'. Record time and commission/omission errors.
Slow completion >90 sec; multiple omissions; poor attentional engagement
Fast but imprecise; multiple false positives; attentional capture
While counting backward by 2s from 30, patient names objects examiner points to. Assess dual-task performance.
Maintains both tasks with ≤2 errors each
Abandons one task; poor cognitive resource sharing; effortful mono-tasking
Switches rapidly between tasks; agitated but inaccurate dual-task performance
Find a target symbol (star) among 20 distractors (circles) on paper. Record time and accuracy.
Finds target in <15 sec; systematic search pattern
>45 sec; unsystematic; gives up; poor attentional sweep
Fast but random; impulsive pointing; misidentifies distractors
Examiner says 'ready' before a stimulus (tap on table). Measure preparatory behavioral response and readiness.
Postural readiness; focused gaze; appropriate anticipatory state
Blunted alerting; no preparatory response; disengaged; psychomotor inertia
Exaggerated startle; over-alerting; anxious anticipatory response
Valid/invalid arrow cue (drawn on paper) before peripheral target. Note accuracy and reaction time difference.
Orienting benefit ≥100 ms for valid cues; accurate
Absent orienting benefit; poor spatial attention direction
Hyper-orienting; inability to disengage from cued location; perseverative attention
Cancel all circles on a sheet with mixed shapes (circles, squares, triangles). Speed and accuracy.
Completes in <60 sec; ≤2 omissions or false cancellations
Slow; >120 sec; multiple omissions; poor inhibitory drive
Fast but with multiple false cancellations; impulsive cancelling of non-targets
Letter cancellation for 5 min. Compare hit rate first 2.5 min vs second 2.5 min. Calculate fatigue index.
Performance drop <10% between first and second half
>30% decline; rapid attentional fatigue; poor sustained engagement
No decline but accelerating speed with increasing errors; agitated performance

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.title: Medication & Clinical Notes
network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.

rows: Ask: 'When trying to sleep, do thoughts race or repeat in your mind?' Rate 0-10.
0-3; mind quiets at bedtime; DMN deactivates normally
0; no pre-sleep rumination; early insomnia or denial
≥7; racing thoughts at bedtime; DMN HYPER hallmark; cognitive hyperarousal insomnia
Ask 3 personal memories including 1 about a good night's sleep vs worst sleep. Note emotional content.
Specific memories; balanced; good sleep memories accessible
Sparse; poor recall; sleep deprivation impairs memory encoding
Good sleep memories inaccessible; 'can't remember sleeping well'; insomnia identity
Ask: 'Has insomnia become part of who you are?' Rate 0-10.
≥8; insomnia as core identity; catastrophizing; 'I am an insomniac'
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note: is off-task content sleep-related?
0/4; hypervigilant task focus; suppression or very mild insomnia
3-4/4 off-task; sleep-related worries during daytime; insomnia DMN generalization
Ask: 'What do you think will happen if you don't sleep well tonight?' Rate catastrophic thinking 0-10.
0-3; realistic; accepts occasional poor sleep; no catastrophizing
0; no concern; does not monitor sleep; early insomnia
≥7; catastrophic predictions; 'I'll fail tomorrow'; 'I'll get sick'; DMN HYPER insomnia
Forgets; sleep deprivation impairs prospective memory
Recalls but effortfully; insomnia sleep-deprivation cognitive impact
Ask: 'Tell me about your sleep patterns over the past month.' Rate coherence and catastrophic framing.
Minimizes sleep problem; avoidant; poor self-monitoring
Catastrophic framing; every night described as disaster; insomnia DMN narrative
Backward digit recall. Note performance variability.
Span ≤3; sleep deprivation working memory failure
Slow >150 sec; sleep deprivation executive function failure
90-150 sec; moderate; insomnia sleep deprivation CEN slowing
7-9 words; moderate; sleep deprivation cognitive drain
Name ink color. Include sleep-related words (tired, rest, awake). Compare interference.
Slow >90 sec; sleep deprivation executive failure
Selectively slowed on sleep-related words; hypervigilance to sleep content; insomnia CEN
Administer digit span twice (beginning and end of assessment). Compare performance.
Consistent span across sessions; stable CEN function
Low across both; global sleep deprivation failure
Variable; better early then worse late; sleep deprivation attentional fatigue
Tap for all except X. 20 letters.
High omissions; sleep deprivation poor initiation
High commission errors; microsleep-related impulsive response; insomnia
Ask: 'Do you feel physically alert or wired even when tired?' Rate 0-10.
0; no arousal; pure sleep-scheduling insomnia or mild
≥7; physiological hyperarousal despite fatigue; SN HYPER insomnia hallmark
Ask: 'Do you feel more awake/anxious when in your bedroom?' Rate 0-10.
0-3; bedroom associated with relaxation and sleep
0; no bedroom anxiety; behavioral insomnia early stage
≥7; bedroom triggers arousal; conditioned arousal; psychophysiological insomnia
Count heartbeats 25 sec. Note: do patients hypermonitor body for sleep readiness?
Accuracy >0.70; body focus does not trigger arousal
Low accuracy; poor body awareness; not hypervigilant
High accuracy AND reports monitoring body for sleep readiness; SN HYPER insomnia
Ask: 'How often do you check the time when trying to sleep?' Rate 0-10.
≥7; repeated time checking; SN HYPER for sleep cues; insomnia perpetuating behavior
Ask: 'Are you bothered by sounds, light, or temperature when trying to sleep?' Rate 0-10.
≥7; extreme sensory sensitivity at night; SN HYPER; insomnia sensory hyperarousal
Misses both; sleep deprivation monitoring failure
Catches errors; perfectionism; 'can't let it go'; insomnia-anxiety perfectionism
Exaggerated startle; hyperarousal; insomnia SN HYPER baseline arousal
Ask: 'Can you relax your body on demand?' Rate ability to achieve muscle relaxation 0-10.
≥6; able to relax on demand; progressive relaxation accessible
0; not monitored; unaware of body tension
≤3; unable to physically relax; somatic hyperarousal; insomnia SMN HYPER
Palpate/observe trapezius, jaw, and forearms. Rate visible tension 0-10 per region.
Tension ≥7 in ≥2 regions; somatic hyperarousal; insomnia SMN HYPER marker
Normal rate but with fatigue markers; slow blink rate; sleepiness signs
Bilateral grip. Note: is grip weak (fatigue) or tense (hyperarousal)?
Weak; sleep deprivation motor fatigue; HYPO
Normal or above-normal grip; tense; cannot fully release; somatic hyperarousal
Normal but with tremor; physiological hyperarousal in motor output
Multiple failures; sleep deprivation balance and coordination
Normal; mild balance instability; sleep deprivation proprioception effect
Excessive sway; sleep deprivation balance HYPO
Stable but excessive muscle tension; cannot achieve restful stance; somatic HYPER
Hand lift when pen drops. 3 trials.
Highly variable (150-500 ms); microsleep-related lapses; insomnia reaction time variability
Ask: 'Do you feel anxious as bedtime approaches?' Rate anticipatory sleep anxiety 0-10.
0; no sleep anxiety; behavioral or circadian insomnia type
≥7; severe sleep anxiety; Limbic HYPER; psychophysiological insomnia core feature
4-5/6; mild; sleep deprivation reduces emotional face processing speed
Flat; sleep deprivation blunts emotional processing
Appropriate labels but irritability/frustration colors all; sleep deprivation limbic HYPER
10 emotional and 10 neutral words. Recall after 5-min distractor.
Flat recall; sleep deprivation impairs emotional memory encoding
Normal emotional advantage but overall recall reduced; sleep deprivation effect
Rate interest in 5 activities 0-10. Note: is low interest from fatigue or true anhedonia?
Average ≥5; motivation present; fatigue limits but doesn't eliminate
Average ≤2; severe daytime fatigue-driven anhedonia from insomnia
High interest; fatigue prevents engagement; preserved motivation with fatigue barrier
Ask: 'What is the worst thing about not sleeping?' Rate catastrophizing 0-10.
0-3; realistic; accepts occasional poor sleep consequences
0; no beliefs; not a thought-maintaining insomnia pattern
≥7; catastrophic; 'not sleeping will destroy my health/career/life'; limbic HYPER
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no shift; sleep deprivation emotional blunting
Shift achieved but slow; sleep-deprived emotional regulation is sluggish; limbic efficiency HYPO
Ask: 'How likely are you to fall asleep in these situations: watching TV, sitting quietly, in a car?'
Not likely in active situations; normal daytime alertness maintained
No sleepiness; possible circadian or behavioral insomnia type
Very likely in all situations; severe daytime sleepiness; sleep deprivation Epworth proxy
Circle all A's in 20-item letter list. Note performance variability.
Slow; multiple omissions; sleep deprivation attention failure
Variable; normal then fails; microsleep-related attentional lapses
Count backward from 50 for 60 sec. Note: any lapses, pauses, or restarts.
Stops; sleep deprivation sustained attention failure
2-4 lapses; microsleeps; insomnia sustained attention hallmark
Abandons one; sleep deprivation attention resource failure
Performs both with 3-5 errors; sleep deprivation dual-task vulnerability
20-40 sec; systematic but with 1-2 pauses; microsleep interruptions
Show analog clock. Ask what time it is. Note: does patient stare at clock beyond task?
Cannot read clock; sleep deprivation visual attention failure
Reads correctly but stares; clock hypervigilance; SN-VAN HYPER for sleep cues
Variable speed; fast then pauses; microsleep-related; insomnia
Drop >40%; sleep deprivation rapid attentional fatigue
20-35% drop; moderate; insomnia sleep deprivation core attentional feature

rows: Ask: 'Does your mind feel blank or empty when you're not doing anything?' Rate mental blankness 0-10.
0-3; spontaneous thought present; mental activity maintained
0; no brain fog; mild or recovered Long COVID
≥7; pervasive mental blankness; spontaneous thought absent; Long COVID DMN HYPO hallmark
Ask 3 personal memories. Note: are memories accessible or trapped 'behind fog'?
Sparse; very slow; 'can't find them'; brain fog memory retrieval failure
Memories present but take extreme effort; 'fog between me and memories'; Long COVID pattern
10 adjectives with self-reference frame. Recall after 2-min distractor.
Normal recall on good day; fluctuates with symptom severity; Long COVID variability
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note off-task content.
0/4; mental blankness even off-task; no spontaneous cognition
3-4/4 off-task; cannot maintain reading; brain fog attention failure
Ask: 'Do your cognitive symptoms vary day to day or within a day?'
No fluctuation; possibly early/mild Long COVID
Significant daily fluctuation; worse post-exertion (PEM); Long COVID DMN variability
Completely forgets; brain fog prospective memory failure
Recalls but extremely effortful; 'had to work very hard to remember'; Long COVID
Ask: 'How has Long COVID changed your daily life?' Rate coherence and emotional content.
Coherent; realistic adjustment; some positive coping
Overwhelmed narrative; profound disability; grief over lost function; Long COVID
Backward digit recall. Maximum span. Note effort vs performance.
Span ≤3; brain fog working memory failure; Long COVID CEN HYPO
Span 4; effortful; 'harder than it should be'; Long COVID CEN
Slow >150 sec; multiple errors; Long COVID CEN failure
90-150 sec; moderate slowing; brain fog CEN vulnerability
Copy symbols under numbers for 60 sec. Count correct pairs.
<20 pairs; severe processing speed failure; Long COVID brain fog core
20-39 pairs; mild-moderate; brain fog processing speed
6-9 words; reduced; Long COVID brain fog
Name ink color. Time and errors.
Slow >90 sec; brain fog executive failure
45-75 sec; moderate slowing; Long COVID processing speed + executive
3-4/5; mild errors; brain fog CEN involvement
Tap for all except X. 20 letters.
High omissions; brain fog poor initiation
2-4 errors; mild; brain fog response inhibition
Ask: 'Do you feel dizzy or worse when standing?' Rate orthostatic symptoms 0-10.
0; no dysautonomia; mild or recovered Long COVID
≥6; orthostatic intolerance; Long COVID autonomic SN dysfunction
Rate discomfort with light, sound, smell 0-10 each.
All ≤2; sensory blunting; severe or atypical Long COVID
≥7 in ≥2 modalities; sensory hypersensitivity; Long COVID SN HYPER
Low accuracy <0.50; poor body awareness despite symptoms; dissociation
High accuracy but hyperaware of cardiac symptoms; Long COVID health anxiety
Ask: 'Do symptoms worsen after physical or mental effort?' Rate PEM severity 0-10.
0-2; normal fatigue after exertion; no PEM
0; no PEM; mild or early Long COVID
≥7; significant PEM; hallmark Long COVID SN-autonomic feature
Misses both; brain fog impairs ACC error monitoring
Catches errors; significant distress; 'can't believe I missed that'; Long COVID mood
Alternate visual and auditory tasks. Note symptom increase with switching.
Fast but triggers headache or cognitive fatigue; Long COVID SN overload
Absent orienting; mental fog; blunted response
Exaggerated; triggers cognitive fatigue; sensory overload
Finger tapping 10 sec each hand. Then wait 2 min and repeat. Compare pre-post exertion.
≥45 taps; no significant reduction on repeat
<35 taps at baseline; severe Long COVID motor impairment
Normal baseline; ≥20% decline on repeat; post-exertional motor fatigue; Long COVID SMN
Bilateral grip. Perform 3 repetitions. Note fatigue across repetitions.
Firm symmetric; no fatigue across 3 reps
Weak bilateral; Long COVID motor weakness
Normal first grip; significant reduction by third; Long COVID motor fatigability
Multiple failures; Long COVID motor ataxia or extreme fatigue
Normal but very slow; highly cautious; Long COVID deconditioning pattern
Excessive sway; Long COVID autonomic/proprioceptive involvement
Stable but patient reports dizziness; dysautonomia contribution to balance
Normal first 5 sec; slows last 5 sec; Long COVID motor fatigability
Ask: 'How much walking before symptom worsening?' Rate activity tolerance 0-10.
≥7; able to perform normal daily activities
≤2; severe activity intolerance; Long COVID disability
3-5; moderate activity intolerance; Long COVID deconditioning + PEM
Hand lift when pen drops. 3 trials.
250-400 ms; mild slowing; Long COVID processing speed and motor
Ask: 'Do you feel more anxious or worried about your health since Long COVID?' Rate 0-10.
0; denial; suppression; minimizing Long COVID symptoms
≥7; significant health anxiety; Long COVID neuroinflammatory limbic HYPER
4-5/6; mild; brain fog reduces emotional processing efficiency
Appropriate labels but effortful; 'brain fog makes it hard to find the word'
Rate interest in 5 activities 0-10. Note: is low interest from anhedonia or fatigue barrier?
Average ≥5; motivation present; activity limited by symptoms not interest
Average ≤2; Long COVID anhedonia; neuroinflammatory limbic HYPO
High interest but symptoms prevent engagement; preserved motivation with functional barrier
10 emotional and 10 neutral words. Recall after 5-min distractor.
Flat recall; brain fog impairs emotional memory encoding
Normal emotional advantage but overall recall reduced; Long COVID memory-emotion
3 health-related and 2 neutral scenarios. Rate anxiety 0-10.
Appropriate 2-5 for health scenarios; not catastrophic
≥8 on health scenarios; catastrophic health anxiety; Long COVID limbic HYPER
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no shift; Long COVID emotional blunting
Stuck in negative mood; brain fog prevents positive affect access; Long COVID depression
Ask: 'How often does your mind go blank mid-task?' Rate 0-10.
0; no brain fog; mild or recovered
≥7; frequent mid-task blank outs; Long COVID brain fog attention hallmark
Circle all A's in 20-item letter list. Note effort and errors.
Slow; multiple omissions; brain fog selective attention failure
Completes but 60-90 sec; effortful; 'harder than it should be'; Long COVID
Abandons one; brain fog attentional resource failure
Performs both but 4-6 errors each; Long COVID dual-task vulnerability
20-45 sec; systematic but very slow; brain fog processing speed
Blunted; not ready; brain fog alerting HYPO
Normal alerting but immediately fatigued; Long COVID sustained alerting failure
After 10 min of assessment: rate cognitive fatigue, headache, and dizziness 0-10 each.
All ≤3; no symptom increase with cognitive effort
No increase; mild or recovered Long COVID
≥5 on ≥2 measures; cognitive PEM; Long COVID hallmark response to mental exertion
Normal start; significant slowing by end; fatigue-driven inhibition failure
Drop >50%; extremely rapid brain fog attentional fatigue; Long COVID hallmark
20-40% drop; moderate; Long COVID attentional fatigue core complaint

rows: Ask: 'Do you feel mentally exhausted by tasks that shouldn't be tiring?' Rate cognitive fatigue 0-10.
Fatigue ≤1; denial; stoic suppression; or very early MS
≥7; disproportionate cognitive fatigue; MS-specific DMN HYPO marker
3 personal memories. Note slowed retrieval speed and coherence.
Sparse; very slow; poor retrieval; moderate-advanced MS cognitive
Memories intact but retrieval extremely effortful; MS cognitive fatigue
10 adjectives with self-reference frame. Recall after 2-min distractor.
Normal recall but very slow; processing speed contribution
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note off-task content.
3-4/4 off-task; cognitive fatigue causes task disengagement; MS DMN drift
Completely forgets; MS prospective memory failure
Recalls but very slowly; MS processing speed impairs prospective memory
Ask: 'Tell me about your life with MS.' Rate coherence, fatigue impact, adjustment.
Coherent; adaptive coping; realistic acknowledgment of MS impact
Sparse; flat; MS apathy; poor self-reflection
Overwhelmed; catastrophic framing; focuses entirely on disability
Animals in 1 min. Note retrieval speed and total.
<7; poor fluency; MS semantic network dysfunction
8-11; reduced but present; MS cognitive slowing
Slow >180 sec; multiple errors; MS CEN white matter failure
90-150 sec; MS processing speed reduction; executive vulnerability
Copy symbols under numbers for 90 sec. Count correct pairs.
<25 pairs; MS processing speed core deficit; SDMT criterion
25-44 pairs; mild-moderate MS cognitive impairment; borderline
Name ink color. Time and errors.
Slow >90 sec; severe MS executive failure
45-75 sec; MS processing speed + executive interaction
≤2/5; MS working memory and executive failure
Tap for all except X. 20 letters.
High omissions; poor initiation; MS motor-cognitive interaction
2-4 errors; MS cognitive slowing affects response inhibition
Ask: 'Do you feel a wave of whole-body fatigue distinct from muscle weakness?' Rate 0-10.
0; no MS fatigue; physical fatigue absent or minimal
≥7; disproportionate whole-body fatigue; MS-specific SN-interoceptive fatigue signal
Fingernail pressure bilaterally. Ask about spontaneous pain. Note dysesthesia.
Low bilateral; poor somatic awareness; advanced MS sensory loss
≥7; hyperalgesia; dysesthetic pain; MS central pain syndrome
Accuracy <0.50; MS autonomic dysregulation; sensory pathway failure
High accuracy; hyperaware of MS-related somatic changes
Misses both; cognitive fatigue impairs MS error monitoring
Catches errors; significant distress; perfectionism; MS-depression interaction
Alternate visual tracking and auditory counting. Switch latency.
Slow >5 sec; MS white matter switching failure
2-4 sec switch; mild slowing; MS processing speed
Ask: 'Do hot temperatures worsen your symptoms (Uhthoff phenomenon)?'
No awareness; early MS or remission
Yes; heat causes functional decline; MS SN pathway sensitization
Absent orienting; MS sensory pathway failure or fatigue
Exaggerated; prolonged recovery; MS-fatigue sensory hypersensitivity
Index tapping 10 sec each hand. Note asymmetry and fatigue over trials.
≥45 taps; ≤10% asymmetry; no fatigue across 3 trials
<35 taps; marked slowing; MS corticospinal tract involvement
Normal first trial; significant slowing by trial 3; MS motor fatigue
Bilateral grip. Compare strength and note fatigue.
Firm symmetric; no fatigability on 3 repetitions
Weak bilateral; MS motor pathway failure
Normal first grip; marked reduction on third; MS motor fatigability
Thumb-to-finger 10 sec. Note fatigue and coordination.
≤2; MS fine motor failure; cerebellar or corticospinal
3 sequences; irregular rhythm; mild MS motor involvement
Heel-to-toe 10 steps. Note ataxia and truncal sway.
Cannot perform; cerebellar MS; ataxic gait
2-3 step failures; mild ataxia; cerebellar MS involvement
Feet together eyes closed 30 sec. Note Romberg sign.
Falls; positive Romberg; dorsal column MS lesion; proprioceptive failure
Excessive sway; borderline Romberg; MS sensory pathway involvement
Marked dysdiadochokinesia; cerebellar MS tract involvement
Mildly irregular; early cerebellar MS involvement
Point to examiner's finger then own nose, 5 repetitions. Note intention tremor.
Accurate; no intention tremor; smooth movement
Cannot perform; severe cerebellar MS; dysmetria
Mild intention tremor on approach; early cerebellar MS
Walk 25 feet (7.6 m) at maximum safe speed. Time it. (MS T25FW standard.)
≥20 sec; severely impaired mobility; MS EDSS 5+
Ask: 'How has your mood been since your MS diagnosis?' Rate depression impact 0-10.
≥7; significant depression; limbic HYPER; MS depression comorbidity (50% lifetime)
<4/6; MS emotional recognition failure; periventricular limbic involvement
Ask: 'Do you cry or laugh more easily since MS?' Rate 0-10.
≥7; pseudobulbar affect; bilateral MS corticobulbar tract involvement
Appropriate labels but fatigued; emotional processing effortful with MS
Average ≤2; MS apathy; limbic HYPO; poor rehabilitation engagement
Average ≥6 but fatigue prevents participation; MS fatigue barrier vs anhedonia
≥8 on MS-related scenario; MS anxiety; catastrophic fear of progression
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no shift; MS apathy; emotional blunting
Stuck in sad mood; MS depression contributing to limbic rigidity
Count backward from 50 for 60 sec. Note fatigue-driven performance decline.
Stops within 30 sec; MS attentional failure from fatigue
Consistent but dramatically slows over 60 sec; MS attentional fatigue
Circle all A's in 20-item letter list.
Slow; multiple omissions; MS attention failure
Completes; 45-75 sec; MS processing speed reduction
Paced Auditory Serial Addition Test — Simplified
Add pairs of digits read at 3-sec pace (1+2=? then 2+3=? etc.) for 5 pairs. Score correct.
≤1/5 correct; MS PASAT failure; auditory working memory + processing speed
2-3/5; mild MS cognitive impairment; MS clinical benchmark
Abandons one; MS attentional resource depletion
Performs both; 3-5 errors each; dual-task vulnerability; MS hallmark
Fails; disorganized; MS severe attention failure
20-40 sec; systematic but slow; MS processing speed
Blunted; not ready; MS fatigue-driven alerting HYPO
Normal alerting but rapidly fatigues; MS sustained alerting failure
Arrow cue before target. Orienting benefit.
Absent orienting; MS parietal white matter failure
Present but diminished; MS attention slowing
Drop >40%; rapid MS attentional fatigue; core MS complaint

rows: Ask: 'Do you have unwanted thoughts that feel foreign to you?' Rate frequency and egodystonicity 0-10.
Minimal intrusive thoughts; poor insight; avoidant; or pure-obsessional OCD
≥8 frequency; ≥8 egodystonicity; classic OCD DMN intrusion pattern
3 personal memories. Note: does OCD content (contamination, harm) intrude into memory retrieval?
Specific memories; minimal OCD theme contamination
Sparse; avoidant; poor self-referential processing; suppression
OCD themes contaminate non-related memories; memory as obsessional trigger
Ask: 'Do your OCD thoughts feel like you or not like you?' Rate self-discordance 0-10.
0-3; insight into ego-dystonic nature; healthy ego distance
0; thoughts feel ego-syntonic; OCD-personality overlap; poor insight variant
8-10; extreme ego-dystonicity; thoughts feel like alien intrusions
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note: is off-task thinking OCD-themed?
3-4/4 off-task; OCD-specific obsessional content; DMN-OCD capture
Ask: 'Can you leave a task uncertain or incomplete without checking?' Rate tolerance 0-10.
≥8 tolerance through avoidance of triggering tasks; not adaptive
≤2; extreme uncertainty intolerance; OCD core cognitive signature
5-min reminder task. Note: does patient check repeatedly that they haven't forgotten?
Independent recall within 5±1 min; no checking
Asks repeatedly before 5 min; checking behavior; OCD prospective uncertainty
Observe if patient adjusts objects during assessment. Rate compulsive ordering 0-10.
0; complete indifference to order; contamination-type OCD
≥7; adjusts objects; distress at asymmetry; symmetry/ordering OCD subtype
Backward digit recall. Note: does OCD doubt cause re-checking of sequence?
Normal span but asks to repeat or correct; doubt-driven checking
Alternating connections. Note: does patient retrace completed connections?
Slow >150 sec; poor cognitive flexibility; OCD CEN rigidity
Slow due to retracing and checking; >150 sec from compulsive retracing
Letter fluency 60 sec per letter. Note: does OCD doubt interrupt generation?
Normal count but interrupted by 'does that count?'; rule-checking during fluency
Include contamination or harm words. Compare interference.
Selectively slowed on OCD-relevant words; OCD emotional Stroop
Backward 100-7 for 5 steps. Note: does patient recheck answers?
4-5/5 but asks to 'check'; compulsive rechecking; OCD CEN-checking loop
Tap for all except X. Note: do errors cause extreme distress?
High omissions; avoidant; poor inhibitory initiation
1-2 commission errors cause extreme distress; error hypersensitivity; OCD hallmark
Sort 6 objects by size then color. Switch rule without announcement. Note perseveration.
Detects rule change within 2 trials; <3 perseverative errors
Cannot detect change; extreme perseveration; OCD cognitive rigidity
Detects change but repeats old rule; needs explicit confirmation before switching
Ask: 'Do you feel you've made a mistake even when you know you haven't?' Rate 0-10.
0-1; no error monitoring; poor self-evaluation; insight deficit
≥8; constant error sense; ACC hyperactivation; 'not just right' OCD hallmark
Present 3 tasks (stack books, align pens, close box). Rate discomfort if not perfectly aligned 0-10.
0; complete indifference; contamination OCD; not symmetry-type
≥7 on ≥2 tasks; extreme 'not just right'; SN HYPER OCD signature
Count heartbeats 25 sec. Then ask: 'Are you aware of contamination sensations on your hands?'
Accurate heartbeat count; no contamination hyperawareness
Low accuracy; poor interoception; avoidant suppression
High accuracy AND hyperaware of somatic contamination cues; SN contamination circuit
Ask patient to lock/close something then walk away. Rate return urge 0-10.
No return urge; completion accepted; uncertainty tolerated
No checking urge; avoidant; hasn't engaged with trigger
Strong return urge ≥8; cannot tolerate completion uncertainty; OCD SN hallmark
Ask patient to touch a common object (pen). Rate disgust/contamination discomfort 0-10.
0-3; no contamination sensitivity; tolerates neutral touch
0; complete indifference; other OCD type; suppression
≥7; contamination disgust; hand-washing urge; contamination OCD subtype
Sensory Switching — OCD Doubt on Transition
Alternate visual and auditory tasks. Note: does switching trigger doubt about previous task?
<2 sec switch; smooth; no backward doubt
Slow switching; rigid; poor cognitive flexibility
Switches but immediately doubts previous task; OCD doubt on transition
Rate probability that touching a doorknob will cause illness 0-100%.
≥40%; extreme threat overestimation; harm OCD; SN-ACC hyperactivation
Ask: 'Do you perform repeated physical actions to reduce anxiety (checking, washing, ordering, tapping)?' Rate categories 0-10.
0; pure obsessional OCD; mental rituals only; no motor compulsions
≥5 ritual categories; hours of compulsive motor behavior/day; SMN HYPER
Start writing 5 letters; ask patient to stop mid-sequence. Rate urge to complete 0-10.
Stops; no urge; avoidant or pure obsessional type
Cannot stop; 'must finish'; urge ≥8; compulsive motor completion; corticostriatal loop
Tapping 10 sec each hand. Note: does asymmetry in counts trigger checking?
≥45 taps each; accepts count difference; no checking
Normal tapping but asks for count comparison; adjusts to match; symmetry OCD motor
Count skin picking, nail checking, object adjustment, hair touching during 10-min assessment.
≥5 repetitive self-directed behaviors per 10 min; motor compulsive pattern; OCD SMN
Heel-to-toe 10 steps. Note: does step asymmetry trigger compulsive re-stepping?
Steps off then re-does; symmetry OCD triggers motor compulsion during gait
Excessive sway; poor proprioception; motor disengagement
Stable but extreme vigilance about body position; hyperaware of sway; interoceptive OCD
Pronate/supinate 10 sec. Note: does patient check rhythm quality?
Regular but asks if rhythm was correct; performance checking; OCD motor doubt
Write name and address. Note: erasing, restarting, extreme dissatisfaction?
Rewrites ≥2 times; extreme perfectionism; compulsive writing; OCD motor
Rate anxiety when NOT performing compulsion after obsession triggers 0-10.
0; no anxiety; pure obsessional or avoidant; no compulsions
≥8; intolerable anxiety; driving all compulsive behavior; limbic HYPER OCD core
6 emotions from photos. Note threat/disgust biases.
<4/6; emotional recognition failure; OCD emotional blunting
Misidentifies neutral as disgusted/fearful; emotion misattribution; OCD threat bias
Rate disgust to 5 scenarios (touching trash, raw meat, spider, stranger's pen, public toilet) 0-10 each.
Average ≤2; disgust blunting; not contamination OCD type
Average ≥7; extreme disgust sensitivity; contamination OCD limbic HYPER
3 scenarios including 1 OCD-adjacent. Name emotional response.
Extreme disgust/anxiety on OCD scenario; disproportionate emotional magnitude
Anxiety ≤5; proportionate to actual harm risk
≥8; catastrophic; hyperresponsive to all harm-adjacent scenarios; harm OCD limbic
Average ≤3; OCD has consumed hedonic capacity; limbic HYPO from exhaustion
High interest expressed but OCD rituals prevent engagement; barrier vs anhedonia
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no affect shift; OCD-emotional numbing
Sad memory triggers OCD rumination; positive memory contaminated by OCD doubt
Count backward from 50 for 60 sec. Note: does patient check progress or restart after errors?
Makes 1 error; restarts from 100; compulsive restart; OCD attention-checking loop
Circle all A's in 20-item list. Include OCD-relevant word. Note attentional capture.
Slow; multiple omissions; poor attentional engagement
Slows near OCD-relevant word; attentional capture; VAN HYPER for OCD cues
Abandons one; rigid; cannot divide attention
Normal performance but demands certainty in each task; cannot tolerate dual-task uncertainty
Find star among 20 distractors. Note: does patient continue searching after finding target?
Fails; gives up; poor attentional drive
Finds target but continues checking to 'make sure'; OCD attentional over-checking
Observe: is patient in heightened readiness for OCD triggers throughout assessment?
Chronically hyper-alerted; scanning for triggers; OCD hypervigilance throughout
Show OCD-relevant image (dirty object). Time to voluntarily look away.
Looks away within 5 sec on request; smooth disengagement
Immediate avoidant gaze aversion; cannot look; avoidant OCD
Cannot look away for >15 sec; attentional lock; OCD SN-VAN capture
Cancel circles on mixed-shape sheet. Note: does patient double-cancel items?
Normal time but double-cancels ≥3 items; compulsive checking during task
Drop >40%; rapid attentional fatigue; OCD cognitive exhaustion
Performance maintained but checking behavior increases over time; compensatory

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.

rows: Ask patient to briefly describe their understanding of the trauma. Rate coherence, fragmentation, integration.
Coherent, integrated account; temporal distance; some distress acceptable
Flat, emotionally absent account; numbing; avoidant PTSD phenotype
Highly fragmented; present-tense re-experiencing; unable to narrate as past event
Ask 3 personal memories: pre-trauma, neutral recent, positive. Rate retrieval vs trauma contamination.
Pre-trauma memories intact; non-trauma memories accessible
Unable to access non-trauma memories; trauma colonizes autobiographical recall
Intrusions of trauma content into unrelated memory retrieval; overgeneral memory
Ask: 'How has your sense of who you are changed since the trauma?' Rate shift 0-10.
0; no self-change sense; denial; avoidant; poor self-awareness
≥8; profound identity destruction; 'I died'; depersonalization; complex PTSD
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note: is off-task content trauma-related?
0/4; hypervigilant task focus; suppression; avoidant phenotype
Ask: 'Where are you right now? What day is it? Are you safe?' Rate grounding 0-10.
Fully oriented; sense of safety; grounded in present
Partially disoriented during distress; dissociative state
Normal orientation but cannot feel safe; pervasive threat sense; hyperarousal PTSD
Trauma intrusion interrupts task; re-experiencing mid-assessment
Ask: 'What do you hope for in the next year?' Rate ability to imagine positive future.
'I don't know'; avoidant; inability to imagine future
'Nothing good will happen'; foreshortened future; PTSD criterion E
Backward digit recall. Note: does stress trigger intrusion?
Span ≤3; PTSD hyperarousal impairs working memory
Normal span but drops when stressed; context-dependent WM failure
Normal but errors increase with duration; sustained CEN failure under pressure
<6; poor verbal output; CEN HYPO; avoidant processing
Include trauma-relevant words. Compare interference vs neutral.
Uniform time; ≤3 errors across all word types
No Stroop effect; emotional numbing; dissociative PTSD
Selectively slowed on trauma words; trauma-specific Stroop effect
Tap for all except X. 20 letters.
High omissions; avoidant PTSD; poor initiation
High commission errors; hypervigilant impulsive; hyperarousal PTSD
Normal but trauma associations contaminate word choices; semantic intrusion
Ask: 'Are you constantly on alert for danger even when safe?' Rate 0-10.
≥8; pervasive threat-readiness; cannot experience safety; SN HYPER hallmark
Drop pen unexpectedly mid-assessment. Rate startle magnitude 0-10, recovery time, and secondary emotional response.
Mild startle; recovery <5 sec; returns to calm
Absent startle; blunted response; dissociative PTSD; numbing
Extreme startle ≥8; prolonged recovery >15 sec; secondary anger/fear; PTSD criterion D
Low accuracy <0.50; body disconnection; dissociative PTSD
High accuracy; hypervigilance to heartbeat; palpitation focus; somatic hyperawareness
Rate threat in 3 ambiguous + 1 objectively safe scenario.
Flat low ratings; numbing; emotional blunting; dissociation
Safe scenario rated ≥6; pervasive threat misidentification; PTSD SN hallmark
Rate sensitivity to sounds, smells, touch 0-10 each.
All ≤2; sensory blunting; numbing PTSD phenotype
≥7 in ≥2 modalities; multi-modal sensory hypersensitivity; SN HYPER PTSD
Catches errors; secondary shame; self-critical; perfectionism PTSD
Low ≤2; pain numbing; dissociation; freeze response
≥8; pain hypersensitivity; somatic PTSD; SN-pain HYPER
Drop pen unexpectedly. Measure: body movement extent, time to stillness, vocalizations.
Small controlled flinch; still within 2 sec; no vocalization
No motor response; freeze PTSD; motor numbing; dissociation
Large whole-body startle; ≥5 sec to stillness; vocalizes; PTSD hyperarousal motor
Observe posture throughout assessment. Rate visible tension in shoulders, jaw, hands 0-10.
Relaxed posture; ≤3 tension in observable areas
Flat; collapsed posture; frozen stance; hypoarousal PTSD
Rigid tense posture; clenched hands; jaw tension; defensive body posture
<35; psychomotor slowing; freeze state; dissociative PTSD
Normal but highly irregular; tremor; sympathetic arousal interfering with motor
Bilateral grip. Note anxiety during close physical proximity to examiner.
Firm symmetric; comfortable with examiner proximity
Weak; avoids examiner contact; hypervigilance to proximity
Grips tightly; difficulty releasing; freeze-grip; hyperactivated protective motor
Heel-to-toe 10 steps. Note environmental scanning during walking.
Multiple failures; dissociated; poor proprioceptive engagement
Normal walking but scans environment; hypervigilant orienting during movement
Hypertense stance; minimal sway but extreme muscle activation; exhausting to maintain
Slow; reduced amplitude; freeze state motor HYPO
Normal rate but visible tremor; sympathetic activation interfering with smooth output
Hand lift when pen drops. 3 trials.
≥500 ms; freeze motor delay; hypoarousal PTSD
<150 ms; false starts; hypervigilant anticipatory motor readiness; PTSD
Identify 6 emotions from photos. Note: neutral faces misidentified as threatening?
Misidentifies neutral as angry/fearful; threat-bias; amygdala HYPER
Rate fear in 2 objectively threatening and 2 objectively safe scenarios.
Threatening ≥6; safe ≤3; discriminates threat from safety
Safe scenarios rated ≥6; fear generalization; hippocampal context failure; PTSD
Accurate differentiated labels; tolerates trauma-adjacent scenario
Extreme reaction on trauma-adjacent scenario; intense limbic reactivity; re-experiencing
10 words (5 trauma-related, 5 positive). Recall after 5-min distractor.
Flat recall; emotional numbing; dissociative PTSD
Trauma words exclusively recalled; positive words inaccessible; PTSD memory bias
Average ≤2; severe anhedonia; limbic HYPO; complex PTSD
Average ≥6 but accompanied by guilt for enjoying; survivor guilt
Ask: 'Are you more irritable or have sudden anger since the trauma?' Rate 0-10.
0-3; normal emotion regulation; no marked change
≥8; explosive anger; limbic HYPER; PTSD criterion D2
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no shift; emotional blunting; dissociation
Sad memory triggers trauma re-experiencing; cannot access happy memory
Count backward from 50 for 60 sec. Note intrusion interruptions.
Stops 1-3 times due to intrusive thoughts; PTSD attentional intrusion
Letter cancellation; include 3 trauma-relevant words embedded. Note time near those words.
Selectively slows near trauma words; attentional capture; PTSD VAN HYPER
Circle all A's in 20-item letter list.
Normal speed but with intrusion breaks; attention disrupted by re-experiencing
Abandons one; avoidant; poor resource allocation
Completes but hypervigilance to examiner consumes attentional resources
Fails; gives up; poor attentional engagement
Fast but hypervigilantly scans beyond found target; cannot disengage search
Absent alerting; no preparatory response; dissociation
Chronically over-alerted; cannot down-regulate vigilance; PTSD hyperarousal
Count orienting responses to new stimuli (movement, door sound) during 10-min assessment.
≤2 orienting per 10 min; returns to task quickly
≥6 orienting per 10 min; hypervigilant scanning; PTSD criterion D
Drop >40%; rapid attentional depletion; chronic PTSD
20-30% drop; moderate; hyperarousal sustaining at cost of accuracy

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.title: Medication & Clinical Notes
network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.

rows: Ask: 'Do you notice any weakness or difficulty since your stroke?' Compare to objective exam findings.
Full awareness; appropriate insight and adjustment
Denies deficits despite clear objective findings; anosognosia; right hemisphere stroke marker
Over-reports deficits beyond objective findings; catastrophic awareness; depression
Draw clock showing 10:10. Score /4. Observe hemispatial neglect pattern.
<2/4; all numbers on one side; hemispatial neglect; right parietal stroke
3/4; mild spatial error; mild planning deficit; anterior stroke
Ask 3 personal memories. Note: intact remote vs impaired recent; peri-stroke amnesia is expected.
Intact pre-stroke memories; peri-stroke gap is normal; integrating post-stroke
Remote and recent memory loss; global amnesia; bilateral hippocampal involvement
Confabulates peri-stroke events; fills memory gaps with invented content
Ask to rate pre-stroke vs current abilities in 3 domains (walking, reading, memory).
Accurate relative self-assessment; appropriate grieving of loss
No recognition of change; anosognosia; right hemisphere stroke
Catastrophically overestimates loss; severe post-stroke depression
Date, year, season, building, city, state. Score /6.
Correct but slow; post-stroke processing speed reduction
Ask patient to describe their stroke experience and goals for recovery.
Coherent narrative; realistic goals; appropriate emotional content
Sparse; flat affect; post-stroke apathy; poor self-reflection
Highly catastrophic; no future goals; post-stroke depression
Animals in 1 min. Note word-finding pauses or circumlocutions.
<8; word retrieval failure; left hemisphere stroke; anomia
12-14 with circumlocutions; anomia; left posterior stroke
Backward digit recall. Maximum span achieved.
Span ≤3; working memory failure; frontal or parietal stroke
Normal span but very slow; processing speed reduction; white matter
Alternating number-letter connections. Time and errors.
Unable to alternate; loses rule; frontal lobe stroke
Slow >150 sec; self-corrected errors; executive slowing post-stroke
Letter fluency 60 sec per letter. Count unique words.
<5; left frontal stroke; Broca area involvement
7-9 with word-finding pauses; left perisylvian stroke
Cannot perform; language comprehension failure; left hemisphere stroke
Slow; multiple self-corrections; post-stroke executive slowing
≤2/5; post-stroke arithmetic failure; left parietal involvement
Tap for all except X. 20 letters at 1/sec.
High omissions; motor aphasia or frontal HYPO
High commission errors; frontal disinhibition post-stroke
5-8 alternations; mildly impaired; post-stroke CEN slowing
Read sentence with 2 errors. Note spontaneous self-correction.
Misses both; anosognosia; no self-monitoring; right stroke
Catches errors; becomes significantly distressed; depression amplifier
Light fingernail pressure bilaterally. Compare sides. Note hemisensory loss.
Absent or markedly reduced one side; sensory stroke; thalamic or cortical
Allodynia; post-stroke central pain; hyperalgesia on affected side
Accuracy <0.50; insular stroke; poor autonomic awareness
High accuracy but hypervigilant; post-stroke health anxiety
Flat ratings; poor threat detection; post-stroke behavioral HYPO
All rated ≥8; catastrophic appraisal; post-stroke anxiety
Alternate visual tracking and auditory counting. Switch latency.
Slow >5 sec; post-stroke switching failure; executive-attentional
Absent orienting; flat affect post-stroke; or advanced impairment
Exaggerated startle; autonomic arousal; post-stroke hypervigilance
Exaggerated; emotional lability; post-stroke emotional dysregulation
Bilateral grip: squeeze examiner's fingers. Compare paretic vs intact hand.
Marked weakness or paresis on affected side; corticospinal stroke marker
Spastic over-grip; cannot release; upper motor neuron spasticity
Index tapping 10 sec each hand. Compare paretic vs intact side.
<25 taps on paretic side; severe motor deficit; corticospinal involvement
Normal rate but irregular rhythm; mirror movements; motor cortex reorganization
Thumb-to-finger opposition each hand separately 10 sec.
≤1 sequence paretic side; finger individualization failure; M1 stroke
Heel-to-toe walk 10 steps. Note lateralized failures.
Cannot perform; hemiparetic gait; requires support
Compensatory wide-base; slow; safety assessment needed
Feet together eyes closed 30 sec. Note one-sided sway.
Falls or requires support; balance system involvement
Asymmetric sway toward paretic side; proprioceptive loss
Dysdiadochokinesia on affected side; cerebellar or motor involvement
Slow irregular bilaterally; cerebellum or basal ganglia involvement
Draw circles with paretic hand. Note size, pressure, motor quality.
Cannot hold pen; severe motor deficit; acute stroke
Abnormal pen grip; compensatory pressure; early motor recovery attempt
Walk 3 meters at comfortable pace. Time it.
≥10 sec (<0.3 m/sec); severely limited; assistance required
5-8 sec; limited community mobility; rehabilitation target
Ask: 'In the past 2 weeks, have you felt down or hopeless, or lost interest in things?' Rate 0-6.
0: Flat; no emotional response; apathy; left anterior stroke
4-6: Moderate-severe depression; limbic HYPER; left anterior stroke risk
4-5/6 with hesitation; bilateral or right posterior involvement
Ask: 'Do you cry or laugh more easily than before your stroke?' Rate 0-10.
0: Complete emotional flat affect; apathy; left anterior stroke
≥7: Pseudobulbar affect; involuntary laughing/crying; bilateral corticobulbar tract
Extreme emotional labels; post-stroke emotional dysregulation
Rate motivation to attend therapy and complete exercises 0-10.
≤3; apathy; post-stroke limbic failure; poor prognosis
High motivation but catastrophic fear of failure; performance anxiety
Rate comfort in 5 social scenarios since stroke 0-10.
≤3 all scenarios; severe withdrawal; post-stroke depression
≤3 in public only; social anxiety; self-consciousness about visible deficits
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no affect change; post-stroke apathy
Stuck in sad mood; cannot shift; post-stroke depression
Count backward from 50 for 60 sec. Note fadeouts.
Frequent pauses; severe post-stroke attention deficit
Very slow; processing speed reduction; white matter involvement
Mark midpoint of a 20 cm horizontal line. Measure deviation from true center.
Deviation >2 cm right (left neglect); right parietal stroke; DAN HYPO
Deviation ±1.5 cm; mild bias; recovering attention system
Cross out all target letters on a sheet. Count omissions per visual field.
≥8 omissions one side; hemispatial neglect; right parietal stroke
3-5 omissions one side; mild attentional bias; early recovery
Count backward by 2s while naming pointed objects.
Abandons one task; post-stroke attention narrowing
Both performed but at reduced speed; dual-task vulnerability
Searches one visual field only; neglect; right parietal stroke
Slow >40 sec; systematic but effortful; attentional recovery
Blunted alerting; poor readiness; post-stroke alerting HYPO
Arrow cue before target. Orienting benefit and disengagement speed.
Absent orienting; parietal failure; neglect side
Deviation toward lesion; neglect on disengagement; parietal stroke
Letter cancellation 5 min; compare first vs last 2.5 min.
Consistent slowing; flat trajectory; processing speed limitation

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.title: Medication & Clinical Notes
network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.
