rows: Ask patient to describe the TBI event and recovery. Note coherence, gaps, temporal sequence.
Coherent narrative; appropriate emotional processing; peri-injury gap is expected
Sparse or absent narrative; poor self-awareness; anosognosia; frontal TBI
Highly distressing; intrusive re-experiencing; PTSD-TBI overlap
3 personal memories (pre-injury, recent, positive). Rate specificity and accuracy.
Pre-injury memories intact; peri-injury gap normal; post-injury integrating
Retrograde amnesia extends before injury; severe DAI pattern
Confabulates injury events; fills memory gaps with plausible but inaccurate content
<2/4; executive-spatial failure; frontal or parietal TBI
10 adjectives with self-reference frame. Recall after 2-min distractor.
Normal recall but dominated by injury-related self-concepts
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note off-task content.
3-4/4; poor sustained attention; TBI DMN-CEN dyscoordination
5-min reminder task. Note if recalled independently.
Completely forgets; TBI prospective memory failure
Asks repeatedly before time; compensatory over-monitoring; anxiety
Backward digit recall. Maximum span achieved.
Slow >150 sec or multiple errors; frontal white matter TBI
Copy 3 symbols under 3 number pairs; complete 10 pairs in 60 sec. Count correct.
<4/10; severe processing speed impairment; TBI core CEN deficit
6-9 words; mild reduction; processing speed contribution
Name ink color. Time and errors.
Slow >90 sec; severe interference; frontal TBI
3-4/5; mild errors; processing speed and attention interaction
Tap for all except X. 20 letters.
High commission errors; frontal disinhibition; TBI
2-4 commission errors; borderline inhibitory control; mTBI
Rate current headache, photophobia, and phonophobia 0-10 each.
0 on all; complete sensory blunting; severe TBI or dissociation
≥6 on ≥2 measures; post-traumatic sensitization; SN HYPER
Observe in normally lit room; ask about discomfort with ceiling lights.
No sensitivity; sensory blunting; severe TBI
Significant discomfort; light avoidance; post-concussion SN hyperactivation
High accuracy; hypervigilant to somatic signals; post-TBI health anxiety
Extreme distress on errors; perfectionism; post-TBI emotional dysregulation
Alternate visual and auditory tasks. Note if switching triggers nausea/pain.
Fast but triggers vestibular symptoms; sensory overload; post-concussion TBI
Fingernail pressure 3 sec. Rate and compare bilaterally.
Low or absent; sensory loss; focal TBI
High bilateral ≥8; central sensitization; diffuse TBI
Unexpected tone mid-task. Measure startle magnitude and recovery.
Exaggerated startle; prolonged recovery >15 sec; PTSD-TBI overlap
Index tapping 10 sec each hand. Count and note rhythm.
<35 taps; motor slowing; DAI or focal motor TBI
35-45 taps; mild reduction; processing speed contribution
Asymmetric weakness; focal motor pathway TBI
Normal but slow force generation; psychomotor slowing
≤2; motor-sequencing failure; cerebellar or frontal TBI
2-3 sequences; mild-moderate fine motor impairment; mTBI
Tandem stance eyes closed 20 sec. Count sway or steps off.
Cannot maintain; falls; vestibular or cerebellar TBI
2-3 adjustments; mild balance impairment; post-concussion vestibular
Walk 5 m at normal pace. Observe stride length, arm swing, stability.
Symmetric; normal stride; bilateral arm swing
Hemiparetic gait; step asymmetry; focal motor TBI
Mildly reduced stride; cautious gait; post-concussion vestibular
Slightly irregular; mild rhythm disruption; cerebellar vulnerability
Cannot hold pen; severe motor TBI
Slight irregularity; fine motor vulnerability post-TBI
Hand lift when pen drops. 3 trials averaged.
≥500 ms; severe processing speed impairment; DAI marker
250-400 ms; mild-moderate slowing; post-concussion processing speed
Ask: 'Since your injury, do you get angry more easily?' Rate 0-10.
0; emotional blunting; limbic HYPO; severe TBI
≥8; post-traumatic irritability; limbic HYPER; amygdala disinhibition
<4/6; affect agnosia; right hemisphere or amygdala TBI
10 emotional and 10 neutral words. Free recall after 5-min distractor.
Flat recall; emotional memory loss; limbic-hippocampal TBI
Negative emotional words preferentially recalled; PTSD-TBI overlap
Intense labels; explosive emotional responses; dysregulation
Average ≥7 but explosive short engagement; impulse dysregulation
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no shift; emotional blunting; limbic HYPO
Stuck in negative mood; poor positive affect access; post-TBI depression
Count backward from 50 for 60 sec. Note rapid fatigue onset.
Stops within 30 sec; severe attentional failure
Completes but dramatically decelerates; attentional fatigue; post-concussion hallmark
Circle all A's in 20-item letter list. Time and errors.
Slow; multiple omissions; severe post-TBI attention failure
Abandons one task; TBI attention resource depletion
Performs both with 3-5 errors each; dual-task vulnerability; TBI hallmark
Fails to find target; disorganized; severe TBI
20-40 sec; systematic but slow; processing speed and attention interaction
Blunted; not ready; post-TBI alerting failure
Arrow cue before target. Orienting benefit and disengagement.
Absent orienting; spatial attention failure; parietal TBI
Normal orienting; cannot disengage; frontal perseveration TBI
Fast with false alarms; impulsive; frontal inhibitory failure TBI
Letter cancellation 5 min; compare first vs last 2.5 min.
Drop >50%; rapid attentional exhaustion; TBI hallmark
20-35% drop; mild-moderate fatigue; post-concussion syndrome marker

network_assessment::medication_clinical_.title: Medication & Clinical Notes
network_assessment::medication_clinical_.content: ⚠ CRITICAL: CRITICAL: Record medication state at each assessment. Results may differ significantly ON vs OFF medication.

rows: Ask: 'How much time do you spend thinking about your tinnitus per day?' Rate 0-10.
0; no rumination; tinnitus not intrusive; mild tinnitus phenotype
≥3 hours/day; constant tinnitus rumination; DMN HYPER tinnitus colonization; severe distress
Ask: 'Would you say tinnitus has become part of who you are?' Rate 0-10.
0-3; tinnitus acknowledged but not identity-defining; adaptive
0; denies tinnitus impact; suppression or mild tinnitus
≥8; tinnitus as core identity; catastrophizing; 'I am my tinnitus'; DMN colonization
3 personal memories. Note: are memories contaminated by tinnitus-related negative emotion?
Specific memories; appropriate emotional content; tinnitus not central
Sparse; poor memory retrieval; attention from tinnitus impairs encoding
Memories contaminated with tinnitus distress; 'can't remember when it was quiet'
2-min reading with 4 probes. Note: is off-task content tinnitus-related?
0/4; hypervigilant task focus; suppression of tinnitus awareness
Pre-Tinnitus vs Current Quality of Life
Ask patient to rate life satisfaction before vs after tinnitus onset 0-10.
Both ≥6; or appropriate minimal decline; good coping
Pre ≥7; current ≤3; significant QoL decline; tinnitus distress; DMN negative impact
Completely forgets; tinnitus-attentional load impairs prospective memory
Recalls but effortfully; tinnitus attention competition
Ask: 'How has tinnitus changed your daily life?' Rate narrative.
Acknowledges impact; maintains positive elements; adaptive coping
Overwhelmed; 'ruined my life'; no positive elements; catastrophizing; DMN HYPER
Span ≤3; tinnitus-related cognitive interference; CEN HYPO
Span 4; borderline; tinnitus attention competition with working memory
90-150 sec; moderate; tinnitus attentional drain on executive
7-9 words; tinnitus attentional competition with verbal output
Name ink color. Time and errors.
Slow >90 sec; severe tinnitus executive interference
45-75 sec; moderate; tinnitus processing speed competition
Tap for all except X. 20 letters.
Alternating fruits and vegetables for 60 sec.
Normal count; mildly slowed; tinnitus cognitive drain
Ask patient to rate tinnitus loudness right now 0-10.
0; no tinnitus at assessment moment; assessment captures quiet period
≥7; loud tinnitus at assessment; SN HYPER active; most distressing presentation
Ask: 'How much does tinnitus bother you right now?' Rate 0-10.
0-3; tinnitus present but not distressing; SN not over-engaged
0; no distress; habituated; successful SN down-regulation
≥7; highly distressing; SN HYPER; emotional-salience coupling active
In quiet room: ask if tinnitus is louder. In noisy environment: ask if tinnitus is masked.
Tinnitus reduced in noise; normal auditory masking; SN modulation intact
Absent tinnitus in noise; complete masking; mild tinnitus
Tinnitus same in noise; poor masking; SN HYPER; central tinnitus generation
Count heartbeats 25 sec. Note: is patient also hyperaware of tinnitus during body focus?
Accurate heartbeat count; tinnitus in background
Low accuracy; poor interoception; tinnitus-body disconnection
Both heartbeat and tinnitus simultaneously hyperattended; SN dual hyperactivation
Ask patient to perform jaw clenching, neck rotation, and jaw protrusion. Note any tinnitus change.
No tinnitus modulation with jaw/neck movements; pure audiogenic
Tinnitus absent with somatic maneuver; somatic origin
Tinnitus changes (louder or quieter) with jaw/neck; somatic tinnitus; SMN-SN interaction
Misses both; tinnitus-related inattention; poor monitoring
Alternate visual and auditory tasks. Note: does auditory task increase tinnitus awareness?
<2 sec; smooth; auditory task does not worsen tinnitus
Auditory task markedly increases tinnitus loudness; auditory salience hyperactivation
Palpate masseter muscle bilaterally. Note: tenderness, clenching, or TMJ dysfunction.
No tenderness; no TMJ symptoms; normal masticatory function
No TMJ findings; non-somatic tinnitus; audiogenic origin
Bilateral tenderness; TMJ dysfunction; bruxism; somatic tinnitus SMN component
Palpate trapezius and cervical paraspinals. Rate tension 0-10 per side.
Tension ≥7; cervicogenic tinnitus; somatic SMN-tinnitus link
<35 taps; unrelated motor slowing; assess for other pathology
Normal tapping; tinnitus does not affect distal motor; expected finding
Weak grip; unrelated to tinnitus; assess other pathology
Normal grip; tinnitus does not affect hand motor; expected
≤2; unrelated to tinnitus; assess other pathology
Normal; tinnitus does not affect fine motor
Rotate head left then right to end range. Rate range and smoothness.
Full symmetric rotation; no tinnitus change
Reduced range; cervical pathology; assess further
Reduced range with tinnitus modulation; cervicogenic tinnitus SMN component
Normal; tinnitus typically does not affect limb motor function
Hand lift when pen drops. 3 trials.
≥450 ms; tinnitus-related cognitive slowing; brain fog overlap
250-400 ms; mild slowing; tinnitus attentional competition with motor readiness
Ask: 'What emotion does your tinnitus bring up?' Rate emotional intensity 0-10.
0-4; mild annoyance; no significant emotional distress; adaptive coping
0; no emotional response to tinnitus; complete habituation or denial
≥8; extreme distress; despair, anger, or hopelessness about tinnitus; limbic HYPER
4-5/6; mild; tinnitus distress reduces emotional processing capacity
Appropriate labels but tinnitus distress colors all responses with frustration/despair
Ask: 'Has tinnitus caused you to feel hopeless or lose interest in life?' Rate 0-10.
0; no depression; tinnitus not distressing; habituated
≥7; tinnitus-driven depression; limbic HYPER; comorbid major depression
Rate interest in 5 activities 0-10. Note: is avoidance from tinnitus distress or anhedonia?
Average ≤3; tinnitus anhedonia; limbic HYPO from severe distress
High interest but tinnitus prevents quiet activities; preserved motivation with sound barrier
Rate comfort in 5 social scenarios. Note if tinnitus drives withdrawal.
≤3 all scenarios; unrelated depression or severe tinnitus
≤3 in social/noisy scenarios; tinnitus-driven social avoidance; limbic-social withdrawal
Sad then happy memory. Mood shift and recovery.
Flat; no shift; tinnitus-related emotional blunting; depression
Stuck in tinnitus-distress mood; cannot access positive affect; limbic HYPER tinnitus
During assessment: count how many times patient spontaneously mentions or rubs ear (tinnitus-directed behavior).
0-1 tinnitus-directed behaviors in 10 min; background awareness
0; tinnitus fully habituated; attention not captured
≥3 tinnitus-directed behaviors; attentional capture; VAN HYPER for tinnitus signal
Circle all A's in 20-item letter list.
Slow; multiple omissions; tinnitus-related attention failure
Normal but effortful; tinnitus competes for attentional resources
Completes but 3-5 errors each; tinnitus competing for attentional resources
20-40 sec; systematic but slow; tinnitus attentional interference
Perform letter cancellation in quiet room. Note: does quiet environment increase tinnitus awareness and decrease performance?
Normal; quiet room does not worsen performance
No change; tinnitus habituated; quiet not triggering
Worse in quiet; quiet increases tinnitus salience; SN paradoxical quiet sensitivity
Normal time but interrupted by ear-touching; tinnitus-directed behavior
20-35% drop; moderate; tinnitus attentional drain over time

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
ADHD Relevance: Cannot suppress. ADHD is characterised by failure to deactivate DMN during goal-directed tasks. mPFC-PCC connectivity is reduced, allowing task-unrelated thoughts and daydreaming to intrude continuously.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Alzheimer's Relevance: PRIMARY NETWORK IN AD. Amyloid-beta preferentially deposits in DMN hubs (mPFC, PCC, precuneus) decades before symptoms. Episodic memory failure, loss of autobiographical narrative, and identity degradation are direct DMN degeneration signatures.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Anxiety Relevance: HYPER. Chronic worry drives persistent future-oriented catastrophic simulation via mPFC. Excessive self-referential threat processing locks DMN in anticipatory anxiety mode, preventing task engagement.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
ASD Relevance: Atypical social mentalizing. Theory of mind, mentalizing, and social cognition — core DMN functions — are fundamentally different in ASD. mPFC-PCC connectivity is atypical, impairing self-other distinction and social prediction.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Chronic Pain Relevance: HYPER. Pain catastrophising and rumination about pain drive mPFC hyperactivation. Distorted body schema and excessive self-referential processing of pain sensations reflect DMN hyperactivity amplifying the pain experience.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Depression Relevance: HYPER-active. Rumination drives sustained mPFC/PCC hyperactivation with over-processing of negative autobiographical memories. Failure to disengage DMN underlies repetitive negative thinking loops.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Insomnia Relevance: HYPER at bedtime. Pre-sleep rumination and worry loops drive mPFC hyperactivation when attempting sleep. Racing thoughts represent failure to deactivate the DMN, preventing the cortical downscaling required for sleep onset.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Long COVID Relevance: HYPO. Brain fog is characterised by mPFC-PCC disconnection and impaired default mode processing. Concentration lapses, word-finding difficulties, and loss of identity/motivation reflect chronic DMN hypoperfusion and neuroinflammatory disruption.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
MS Relevance: Disrupted by white matter lesion load. DMN hub disconnection causes processing speed reduction and cognitive fatigue episodes. Lesions in PCC/precuneus white matter tracts impair autobiographical and self-referential processing.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
OCD Relevance: Obsession-invaded. Obsessive rumination, mental rituals, and thought-action fusion hijack DMN self-referential processing. Intrusive ego-dystonic thoughts represent DMN content that cannot be suppressed by prefrontal inhibition.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
PTSD Relevance: Trauma-invaded. Trauma-related intrusive memories and flashbacks hijack DMN self-referential processing. Dissociation reflects DMN fragmentation. Peri-traumatic identity disruption persists as DMN reorganisation around trauma narrative.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Stroke Relevance: Disrupted by lesion and diaschisis. Right-hemisphere strokes impair self-awareness (anosognosia) via PCC/precuneus disconnection. Post-stroke depression and identity disruption reflect chronic DMN failure.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Stroke Relevance: Disrupted by lesion and diaschisis. Right-hemisphere strokes impair self-awareness (anosognosia) via PCC/precuneus disconnection. Post-stroke depression and identity disruption reflect chronic DMN failure.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
TBI Relevance: Disrupted by diffuse axonal injury (DAI) affecting PCC-mPFC white matter tracts. Identity disruption, self-monitoring failure, and poor self-awareness of deficits (anosognosia) reflect DMN connectivity breakdown.

clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.title: Network assessment maps patient symptoms to functional brain networks for FNON-based montage selection.
clinical_exam::network_assessment_maps_p.content:
1. Default Mode Network (DMN) — Max Score: 14
Core: Self-referential processing, autobiographical memory | Hubs: mPFC, PCC/Precuneus, Angular gyrus, Hippocampus | EEG sites: Pz, P3/P4, Cz
Tinnitus Relevance: Tinnitus awareness intrusion. Chronic tinnitus hijacks DMN self-referential processing: the sound becomes part of default self-awareness. mPFC hyperactivation to tinnitus-related self-referential cognitions maintains tinnitus distress.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: ADHD Relevance: HYPO. PRIMARY NETWORK IN ADHD. DLPFC underactivation underpins working memory deficits, poor planning, and impaired inhibitory control. Reduced CEN-DMN anticorrelation is the ADHD network signature.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Alzheimer's Relevance: HYPO progressive. DLPFC and PPC involvement increases with disease stage. Executive dysfunction, impaired working memory, and reduced cognitive flexibility emerge as CEN atrophy progresses from MCI to moderate AD.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Anxiety Relevance: Compromised. CEN is engaged in worry maintenance rather than effective problem-solving. DLPFC fails to down-regulate amygdala activation, impairing top-down anxiolytic control over threat appraisal.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: ASD Relevance: Variable. Executive dysfunction is present in many individuals with ASD. Cognitive rigidity (insistence on sameness) reflects CEN inflexibility. Working memory may be preserved for rote material but impaired for social/contextual content.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Chronic Pain Relevance: Compromised. Impaired descending pain modulation from DLPFC reduces conditioned pain modulation (CPM) efficacy. Cognitive interference from pain reduces available CEN resources for working memory and planning.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Depression Relevance: HYPO-active. Impaired DLPFC recruitment reduces top-down regulation of rumination. Working memory and attentional control deficits reflect reduced CEN engagement during emotional challenge.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Insomnia Relevance: Hyperactivated pre-sleep. Unlike most conditions where CEN is hypo-active, in insomnia both CEN and DMN are co-activated at bedtime. This cortical hyperarousal prevents the transition from wake to sleep-onset EEG activity.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Long COVID Relevance: HYPO. Executive function impairment, reduced working memory, and cognitive fatigue reflect DLPFC hypometabolism and neuroinflammatory CEN disruption. SDMT and processing speed tasks are most sensitive to Long COVID CEN impairment.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: MS Relevance: HYPO. Periventricular and frontal white matter lesions disconnect DLPFC from PPC, causing the hallmark MS cognitive syndrome: reduced processing speed, working memory impairment, and cognitive fatigue.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: OCD Relevance: Misdirected. DLPFC is engaged in maintaining and responding to obsessional content rather than adaptive problem-solving. Cognitive inflexibility and inability to shift from obsessional thought reflects aberrant CEN-OFC loops.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: PTSD Relevance: HYPO. Impaired prefrontal executive control over trauma memories prevents extinction of fear responses. Reduced DLPFC activity impairs emotion regulation and inability to contextualise trauma memories as past events.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Stroke Relevance: Disrupted especially with frontal/parietal lesions. Post-stroke executive dysfunction, working memory impairment, and cognitive inflexibility reflect CEN damage or disrupted inter-hemispheric CEN connectivity.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Stroke Relevance: Disrupted especially with frontal/parietal lesions. Post-stroke executive dysfunction, working memory impairment, and cognitive inflexibility reflect CEN damage or disrupted inter-hemispheric CEN connectivity.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: TBI Relevance: HYPO. DLPFC is particularly vulnerable to both focal and blast injury. Executive dysfunction, working memory impairment, and impaired problem-solving reflect disrupted CEN after TBI of any severity.

clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.title: Core: Working memory, planning, cognitive control | Hubs: DLPFC, PPC/IPS | EEG sites: F3/F4, P3/P4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_working_memory_plann.content: Tinnitus Relevance: Effortful suppression. CEN is recruited for effortful attention control to suppress tinnitus awareness. This depletes working memory capacity and contributes to concentration impairment and cognitive fatigue in tinnitus sufferers.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: ADHD Relevance: Dysregulated switching. Impaired SN gating between DMN and CEN produces unstable task engagement. Reward sensitivity and impulse control failures reflect dACC and anterior insula dysfunction.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Alzheimer's Relevance: HYPO. Reduced salience detection underlies apathy, disinhibition, and social appropriateness failures. Anterior insula atrophy impairs interoception; dACC dysfunction contributes to motivation loss and error unawareness.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Anxiety Relevance: HYPER. PRIMARY NETWORK IN GAD. Anterior insula and dACC hyperactivation amplify interoceptive threat signals. Autonomic overdrive (palpitations, sweating, dyspnoea) reflects sustained SN hyperactivation.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: ASD Relevance: Atypical social salience. Social stimuli are not automatically detected as salient. Anterior insula differences produce atypical interoceptive processing. Sensory filtering differences reflect altered SN gating of sensory input.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Chronic Pain Relevance: HYPER. PRIMARY NETWORK IN CHRONIC PAIN. Central sensitisation reflects anterior insula and dACC hyperactivation that amplifies nociceptive salience. Interoceptive error signals perpetuate pain catastrophising.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Depression Relevance: Dysregulated DMN↔CEN switching. Blunted interoception produces anhedonia. Reduced anterior insula activity impairs emotional awareness; dACC dysfunction disrupts motivational salience.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Insomnia Relevance: HYPER. PRIMARY NETWORK IN INSOMNIA. Hyperarousal is the core insomnia mechanism. Anterior insula and dACC maintain wakefulness via threat monitoring of the sleep environment. Conditioned arousal reflects SN sensitisation to bedroom cues.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Long COVID Relevance: Dysregulated. Autonomic dysregulation (POTS, orthostatic intolerance) is a primary Long COVID manifestation reflecting SN-ANS axis dysfunction. Post-exertional malaise (PEM) may involve SN hyperexcitability to metabolic signals.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: MS Relevance: Disrupted. Fatigue-related network switching failure reflects impaired SN function. MS fatigue is partly a SN phenomenon: the network cannot efficiently switch between DMN and task-positive networks under load.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: OCD Relevance: HYPER. PRIMARY NETWORK IN OCD. dACC error-signal hyperactivity generates the persistent sense that things are not right (just-right perception). Anterior insula hyperactivation drives interoceptive discomfort compelling compulsions.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: PTSD Relevance: HYPER. PRIMARY NETWORK IN PTSD. Amygdala hyperactivation drives threat hypervigilance. Anterior insula hyperactivity produces interoceptive hyperarousal. dACC hyperactivity maintains the chronic hyperarousal state.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Stroke Relevance: Asymmetrically disrupted. Right-hemisphere lesions impair the VAN, producing hemispatial neglect. Post-stroke emotional dysregulation and attention allocation failure reflect anterior insula/dACC damage.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Stroke Relevance: Asymmetrically disrupted. Right-hemisphere lesions impair the VAN, producing hemispatial neglect. Post-stroke emotional dysregulation and attention allocation failure reflect anterior insula/dACC damage.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: TBI Relevance: HYPER/dysregulated. Emotional lability, stimulus sensitivity, and impulsivity reflect SN hyperexcitability post-TBI. Post-traumatic stress responses may develop as a SN sensitisation phenomenon.

clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.title: Core: Threat detection, interoception, DMN↔CEN switching | Hubs: Anterior Insula, dACC | EEG sites: Fz, FCz, F3/F4
clinical_exam::core_threat_detection_int.content: Tinnitus Relevance: HYPER. PRIMARY NETWORK IN TINNITUS. Auditory salience overamplification via anterior insula and dACC prevents habituation. The SN flags tinnitus as a significant threat, maintaining emotional distress and preventing acoustic desensitisation.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: ADHD Relevance: HYPER. Hyperactivity and motor overflow reflect excessive SMA/M1 excitability. Fidgeting, motor restlessness, and impaired timing/rhythm reflect disrupted cortico-striatal motor circuits.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Alzheimer's Relevance: Relatively spared early; increasingly affected in moderate-severe AD. Ideomotor apraxia, impaired motor sequencing, and gait changes emerge as parietal-premotor involvement increases with Braak staging.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Anxiety Relevance: HYPER-tension. Chronic muscle tension, tremulousness, motor restlessness, and fidgeting reflect sustained SMA/M1 activation driven by continuous autonomic and limbic overflow into somatic pathways.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: ASD Relevance: Atypical motor. Motor stereotypies, coordination difficulties, motor planning difficulties (developmental coordination disorder overlap), and unusual gait patterns reflect atypical SMN organisation and cortico-cerebellar connectivity.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Chronic Pain Relevance: Reorganised. Pain-avoidance motor patterns, protective guarding, and altered proprioceptive processing reflect chronic SMN reorganisation. Reduced motor cortex excitability is observed in fibromyalgia.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Depression Relevance: HYPO-active. Psychomotor retardation reflects reduced SMA and premotor output. Slowed speech, reduced gesture, stooped posture, and bradyphrenia are direct SMN hypoactivation signatures.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Insomnia Relevance: Tension-held. Chronic muscle tension, jaw clenching, restless leg sensations, and somatic arousal at bedtime reflect SMN activation preventing sleep-state motor relaxation. Body-scan reveals inability to achieve motor stillness.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Long COVID Relevance: HYPO-fatigued. Exercise intolerance, fatigue-related motor decline, reduced grip strength, and post-exertional motor deterioration reflect SMN vulnerability to energy depletion. Gait endurance is limited by PEM threshold.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: MS Relevance: PRIMARY NETWORK IN MS. Lesions anywhere along the corticospinal tract produce motor symptoms. Cerebellar lesions produce ataxia; spinal cord lesions produce spasticity. SMN impairment varies by lesion location.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: OCD Relevance: Compulsion-organised. Repetitive motor rituals, compulsive checking movements, motor tics, and avoidance gestures are SMN activations driven by OFC-caudate-thalamic loop hyperactivity.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: PTSD Relevance: Trauma-encoded. Startle responses, muscle tension, freezing/fight/flight motor activations, and motor agitation are SMN hyperactivation signatures. Somatic trauma memories are stored in sensorimotor circuits.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Stroke Relevance: PRIMARY NETWORK IN STROKE REHAB. Corticospinal tract disruption produces contralateral hemiparesis. Ipsilesional M1 may be hyperexcitable; contralesional M1 hypoexcitable (interhemispheric imbalance model).

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Stroke Relevance: PRIMARY NETWORK IN STROKE REHAB. Corticospinal tract disruption produces contralateral hemiparesis. Ipsilesional M1 may be hyperexcitable; contralesional M1 hypoexcitable (interhemispheric imbalance model).

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: TBI Relevance: Variable by lesion. Vestibular/balance disruption, oculomotor abnormalities, fine motor slowing, and post-concussive motor speed reduction reflect SMN and cerebellar involvement post-TBI.

clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.title: Core: Motor output, somatosensation, proprioception | Hubs: M1, S1, SMA, Premotor | EEG sites: C3/C4, Cz
clinical_exam::core_motor_output_somatos.content: Tinnitus Relevance: Somatic component. Jaw (TMJ) and cervical muscle tension contribute to somatic tinnitus. Auditory-motor integration differences are found in tinnitus. Somatic modulation tests reveal SMN involvement in tinnitus generation.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: ADHD Relevance: Dysregulated. Dopamine-mediated reward pathway disruption drives impulsivity, emotional dysregulation (rejection-sensitive dysphoria), and inability to delay gratification via OFC dysfunction.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Alzheimer's Relevance: HYPO/atrophic. Hippocampal degeneration is the anatomical hallmark; entorhinal cortex is earliest affected. Emotional flattening, apathy, and depression reflect limbic network progressive failure and disconnection.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Anxiety Relevance: HYPER. Amygdala hyperactivation drives sustained fear-state without discrete threat. HPA axis overdrive, reduced hippocampal GABAergic inhibition, and OFC worry-loop maintenance perpetuate the disorder.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: ASD Relevance: Atypical emotional processing. Amygdala hyperreactivity to social stimuli may drive social anxiety. Interoceptive differences produce alexithymia. Sensory sensitivities reflect atypical limbic-sensory integration.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Chronic Pain Relevance: Dysregulated. Emotional amplification of pain via amygdala-insula circuits drives the affective component of chronic pain. Comorbid depression and anxiety reflect limbic network involvement in pain perpetuation.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Depression Relevance: PRIMARY NETWORK IN MDD. Amygdala hyperactivation + OFC dysregulation drive sustained low mood, anhedonia, and emotional dysregulation. Hippocampal atrophy correlates with episode duration.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Insomnia Relevance: HYPER. Sleep anxiety, fear of sleeplessness, and conditioned arousal reflect amygdala hyperactivation to sleep-related cues. OFC-mediated safety learning is impaired, perpetuating the bed-as-threat association.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Long COVID Relevance: Dysregulated. Mood disturbance (depression, anxiety), emotional lability, and loss of motivation are highly prevalent in Long COVID. Neuroinflammatory effects on limbic circuits and HPA axis dysregulation drive these symptoms.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: MS Relevance: Dysregulated. MS depression (50% lifetime prevalence) is the most common psychiatric comorbidity. Emotional lability, pathological laughing/crying, and apathy reflect limbic network demyelination or disconnection.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: OCD Relevance: Dysregulated. OFC-caudate-thalamic circuit hyperactivation is the neurobiological hallmark of OCD. Anxiety driving compulsions and brief relief post-compulsion reflect aberrant limbic-striatal reward/punishment signalling.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: PTSD Relevance: HYPER. PRIMARY CO-NETWORK. Amygdala fear conditioning drives all re-experiencing symptoms. Hippocampal contextualisation failure prevents trauma memories from being stored as non-threatening past events.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Stroke Relevance: Post-stroke depression (30-40%) and emotional lability reflect limbic network disruption. Amygdala involvement or disconnection from prefrontal regulation drives emotional dysregulation and grief response.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Stroke Relevance: Post-stroke depression (30-40%) and emotional lability reflect limbic network disruption. Amygdala involvement or disconnection from prefrontal regulation drives emotional dysregulation and grief response.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: TBI Relevance: Dysregulated. Irritability, aggression, depression, and apathy are among the most common sequelae of TBI. Amygdala hyperreactivity, hippocampal vulnerability, and OFC disruption drive behavioural changes.

clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.title: Core: Affect generation/regulation, reward, stress | Hubs: OFC/mPFC, ACC, Amygdala | EEG sites: Fp1/Fp2, AF3/AF4, Fz
clinical_exam::core_affect_generationreg.content: Tinnitus Relevance: Dysregulated. Tinnitus distress, fear/distress response to tinnitus, depression, and anxiety comorbidity reflect limbic network involvement. Amygdala hyperactivation to the tinnitus signal drives the emotional suffering component.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: ADHD Relevance: HYPO/dysregulated. DAN hypoactivation produces inattention and failure to sustain goal-directed attention. VAN hyperresponsivity causes distractibility to task-irrelevant stimuli (attentional capture).

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Alzheimer's Relevance: HYPO early. Reduced processing speed and sustained attention are among the earliest cognitive changes in MCI-AD transition. DAN disruption contributes to visuospatial navigation failures and getting lost.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Anxiety Relevance: Threat-biased. DAN shows attentional capture by threat-relevant stimuli. VAN hyperresponsivity drives hyperorienting to potential threats. Disengagement from non-threatening stimuli is critically impaired.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: ASD Relevance: Atypical profile. Hyper-focus on restricted interests contrasts with impaired shifting to other stimuli. Over-selective attention to detail (local processing bias) reflects atypical DAN/VAN organisation in ASD.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Chronic Pain Relevance: Hypervigilant to pain. Attentional bias toward pain-related stimuli reflects DAN hyperresponsivity to nociceptive cues. VAN-driven orienting to any bodily sensation perpetuates pain hyperawareness.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Depression Relevance: HYPO-active. Concentration impairment and difficulty sustaining attention reflect DAN hypoactivation. Attentional bias toward negative stimuli is mediated by VAN hyperresponsivity to sad/negative cues.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Insomnia Relevance: Hypervigilant self-monitoring. DAN and VAN are co-activated in insomnia: hypervigilant monitoring of sleep-related internal sensations (ticking clock, own heartbeat, tinnitus) prevents sleep. Reduced sleep perception accuracy (paradoxical insomnia).

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Long COVID Relevance: HYPO. Sustained attention impairment and inability to maintain concentration are core brain fog symptoms. DAN disruption reflects reduced cortical activation capacity. Fatigue-related attention failures worsen with exertion.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: MS Relevance: HYPO. Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT) impairment is the most sensitive marker of MS cognitive change. DAN disruption produces processing speed decline; divided attention is particularly vulnerable to lesion load.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: OCD Relevance: HYPER-obsessional. Attention is locked onto obsessional content (threat/contamination/harm) with severe impairment in disengaging. VAN hyperresponsivity drives orienting to obsessional triggers in the environment.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: PTSD Relevance: Threat-biased. Hypervigilant threat scanning reflects DAN hyperresponsivity to danger cues. VAN over-activation produces exaggerated orienting to trauma-related stimuli. Sustained attention to non-threat is severely impaired.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Stroke Relevance: Frequently disrupted. Right MCA strokes cause hemispatial neglect via DAN/VAN imbalance. IPS and FEF lesions impair voluntary orienting; sustained attention deficits are common in frontal stroke territory.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Stroke Relevance: Frequently disrupted. Right MCA strokes cause hemispatial neglect via DAN/VAN imbalance. IPS and FEF lesions impair voluntary orienting; sustained attention deficits are common in frontal stroke territory.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: TBI Relevance: HYPO. Post-concussive attentional deficits are the most common and persistent cognitive symptom. DAN disruption causes poor sustained attention; processing speed reduction is a hallmark across severities.

clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.title: Core: Sustained/selective attention, orienting | Hubs: DAN: IPS/FEF; VAN: rTPJ/rIFG | EEG sites: P3/P4, PO7/PO8, F7/F8
clinical_exam::core_sustainedselective_a.content: Tinnitus Relevance: Captured. Attentional capture by tinnitus reflects DAN hyperresponsivity to the internal auditory stimulus. VAN over-activation produces automatic orienting to tinnitus. Disengagement from tinnitus attention is a key treatment target.

handbook::partners_tier_version_10_march.title: Partners Tier | Version 1.0 | March 2026
handbook::partners_tier_version_10_march.content:
PARTNERS TIER: This document contains the complete FNON framework including network hypothesis generation, 6-Network Bedside Assessment, S-O-Z-O sequencing, and full FNON Level 1–5 clinical decision pathway for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD). For use by the treating Doctor and authorised SOZO clinical staff only.
For use by the treating Doctor and authorised SOZO clinical staff only
© 2026 SOZO Brain Center. All rights reserved. CONFIDENTIAL.
Table of Contents
Introduction
About This Handbook
This handbook provides a complete step-by-step guide to the SOZO clinical pathway for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) using Functional Network-Oriented Neuromodulation (FNON). It is designed for daily clinical use by the treating Doctor and Clinical Assistants at SOZO Brain Center.
Each chapter corresponds to one stage of the patient journey, from initial appointment scheduling through active treatment and long-term therapy optimisation. The handbook integrates content from the SOZO FNON Clinical Protocol (Document A, v7.0), the FNON Master Assessment Document (v2.0), and the SOZO ADHD Protocol Algorithm.
What is FNON?
Functional Network-Oriented Neuromodulation (FNON) is SOZO's proprietary clinical framework. Unlike conventional approaches that target isolated brain regions, FNON targets functional brain networks  the interconnected circuits that underlie ADHD symptoms.
FNON MODEL: The FNON model follows a five-level clinical decision pathway from phenotyping through to iterative adjustment.
Available Modalities at SOZO
WARNING: Off-Label Disclosure: TPS (NEUROLITH®) use in Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder is INVESTIGATIONAL at SOZO Brain Center. It is not CE-marked or FDA-approved for ADHD. All patients must provide written informed consent inclusive of the off-label TPS disclosure. TPS use is restricted to the prescribing Doctor only.
Roles and Responsibilities
WARNING: GOVERNANCE RULE: No Clinical Assistant may independently modify a treatment plan, change a montage, or adjust parameters without Doctor authorisation.
The Patient Journey at a Glance
Stage 1: Appointment Scheduling
Purpose
The patient schedules an appointment for an initial clinical evaluation and development of a preliminary neuromodulation plan. This first contact sets the tone for the entire clinical relationship and ensures that the patient arrives prepared for a comprehensive assessment.
Step-by-Step Procedure
1.1 Receive Enquiry
When a patient or referring physician contacts SOZO Brain Center, the administrative team collects initial information and determines appointment suitability.
1.2 Collect Preliminary Information
Full name, date of birth, and contact details
Referring physician details (if applicable)
Primary diagnosis (confirmed ADHD or suspected)
Brief description of chief complaints
1.3 Pre-Appointment Instructions
Inform the patient to bring the following to their first visit:
All available medical records and discharge summaries
Brain imaging (MRI / CT scans)
Previous EEG reports (if any)
Neuropsychological test results
Complete and current medication list with dosages and timing
Laboratory results (recent blood work)
1.4 Confirm and Schedule
Confirm appointment date, time, and expected duration (allow 2–3 hours for initial assessment)
Send written confirmation via email with pre-appointment instructions
Send a reminder 24 hours before the appointment
Checklist
☐ Patient contact details recorded
☐ Referral information documented
☐ Pre-appointment instructions sent
☐ Appointment confirmed in scheduling system
☐ Reminder sent (24h before)
CLINICAL TIP: A well-prepared patient makes the assessment more efficient. Emphasise the importance of bringing a complete medication list with exact timing of each dose — medication state significantly affects assessment findings and later treatment planning.
Output
Initial visit scheduled. Patient informed of requirements.
Forms Used
Appointment Scheduling Form (Document 01 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 2: Informed Consent
Purpose
Before any diagnostic or therapeutic procedure is performed, the patient must provide written informed consent. This stage ensures the patient fully understands the nature, risks, benefits, and alternatives of the SOZO FNON neuromodulation programme, including the off-label status of TPS for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD).
Step-by-Step Procedure
2.1 Welcome and Documentation
Upon arrival at the clinic, the patient:
Is welcomed by the administrative or clinical team
Completes required registration documentation
Provides identification and emergency contact details
2.2 Consent Discussion
The treating Doctor (or designated clinician) conducts a structured consent discussion covering:
Nature of treatment — Non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) under the FNON framework, which may include tDCS, TPS, taVNS, and CES
Off-label disclosure — TPS is CE-marked for Alzheimer's disease; its use for ADHD is investigational and off-label
Expected benefits — Potential improvement in motor symptoms, cognitive function, mood, pain, and fatigue (results vary; NIBS is adjunctive, not a replacement for medication)
Risks and side effects — Common (tingling, mild headache, skin redness), uncommon (burning sensation), rare (skin lesion, seizure)
Alternatives — Pharmacological treatment alone, DBS for eligible candidates, physiotherapy, other standard-of-care interventions
Right to withdraw — Participation is voluntary; withdrawal does not affect standard care
Data protection — How clinical data will be collected, stored, and used
2.3 Obtain Signatures
Patient signs the Informed Consent Form
Doctor co-signs confirming the consent discussion took place
Witness signature obtained (if required by local regulation)
WARNING: MANDATORY: No diagnostic or therapeutic procedures may be performed prior to informed consent being obtained. This includes the off-label disclosure for all investigational modalities used in ADHD treatment. Non-compliance is a clinical governance violation.
Off-Label Disclosure Summary
Checklist
☐ Patient registration completed
☐ Consent discussion conducted by Doctor
☐ All modalities explained including off-label TPS status
☐ Patient questions addressed
☐ Consent form signed (patient + Doctor + witness)
☐ Signed consent filed in patient folder
Output
Signed informed consent. Patient cleared for assessment.
Forms Used
Informed Consent Form (Document 02 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 3: Psychological Intake & Psychometric Profiling
Purpose
This stage provides structured phenotyping and baseline quantification. The patient is evaluated by a clinical psychologist or trained clinical assistant who conducts a structured clinical interview and performs psychometric testing using the SOZO Patient Rating System (PRS). The data gathered here directly informs the phenotype identification in Stage 4 and the treatment plan in Stage 6.
Step-by-Step Procedure
3.1 Structured Clinical Interview
Conduct a systematic interview covering:
3.2 Digitise Medical Documentation
Scan or digitally archive all medical documents the patient has brought. Record what was provided in the Documentation Checklist section of the intake form.
3.3 SOZO PRS — Patient Rating System (Baseline)
The SOZO PRS is the standardised psychometric tool used to quantify symptom severity across clinically relevant domains. It establishes the baseline against which all future progress will be measured.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Select relevant domains based on diagnosis and presenting phenotype. Focus only on clinically meaningful domains — do not administer all scales to every patient.

Table of Contents
Introduction
About This Handbook
This handbook provides a complete step-by-step guide to the SOZO clinical pathway for Chronic Pain / Fibromyalgia (CP) using Functional Network-Oriented Neuromodulation (FNON). It is designed for daily clinical use by the treating Doctor and Clinical Assistants at SOZO Brain Center.
Each chapter corresponds to one stage of the patient journey, from initial appointment scheduling through active treatment and long-term therapy optimisation. The handbook integrates content from the SOZO FNON Clinical Protocol (Document A, v7.0), the FNON Master Assessment Document (v2.0), and the SOZO CP Protocol Algorithm.
What is FNON?
Functional Network-Oriented Neuromodulation (FNON) is SOZO's proprietary clinical framework. Unlike conventional approaches that target isolated brain regions, FNON targets functional brain networks  the interconnected circuits that underlie CP symptoms.
FNON MODEL: The FNON model follows a five-level clinical decision pathway from phenotyping through to iterative adjustment.
Available Modalities at SOZO
WARNING: Off-Label Disclosure: TPS (NEUROLITH®) use in Chronic Pain / Fibromyalgia is INVESTIGATIONAL at SOZO Brain Center. It is not CE-marked or FDA-approved for CP. All patients must provide written informed consent inclusive of the off-label TPS disclosure. TPS use is restricted to the prescribing Doctor only.
Roles and Responsibilities
WARNING: GOVERNANCE RULE: No Clinical Assistant may independently modify a treatment plan, change a montage, or adjust parameters without Doctor authorisation.
The Patient Journey at a Glance
Stage 1: Appointment Scheduling
Purpose
The patient schedules an appointment for an initial clinical evaluation and development of a preliminary neuromodulation plan. This first contact sets the tone for the entire clinical relationship and ensures that the patient arrives prepared for a comprehensive assessment.
Step-by-Step Procedure
1.1 Receive Enquiry
When a patient or referring physician contacts SOZO Brain Center, the administrative team collects initial information and determines appointment suitability.
1.2 Collect Preliminary Information
Full name, date of birth, and contact details
Referring physician details (if applicable)
Primary diagnosis (confirmed CP or suspected)
Brief description of chief complaints
1.3 Pre-Appointment Instructions
Inform the patient to bring the following to their first visit:
All available medical records and discharge summaries
Brain imaging (MRI / CT scans)
Previous EEG reports (if any)
Neuropsychological test results
Complete and current medication list with dosages and timing
Laboratory results (recent blood work)
1.4 Confirm and Schedule
Confirm appointment date, time, and expected duration (allow 2–3 hours for initial assessment)
Send written confirmation via email with pre-appointment instructions
Send a reminder 24 hours before the appointment
Checklist
☐ Patient contact details recorded
☐ Referral information documented
☐ Pre-appointment instructions sent
☐ Appointment confirmed in scheduling system
☐ Reminder sent (24h before)
CLINICAL TIP: A well-prepared patient makes the assessment more efficient. Emphasise the importance of bringing a complete medication list with exact timing of each dose — medication state significantly affects assessment findings and later treatment planning.
Output
Initial visit scheduled. Patient informed of requirements.
Forms Used
Appointment Scheduling Form (Document 01 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 2: Informed Consent
Purpose
Before any diagnostic or therapeutic procedure is performed, the patient must provide written informed consent. This stage ensures the patient fully understands the nature, risks, benefits, and alternatives of the SOZO FNON neuromodulation programme, including the off-label status of TPS for Chronic Pain / Fibromyalgia (CP).
Step-by-Step Procedure
2.1 Welcome and Documentation
Upon arrival at the clinic, the patient:
Is welcomed by the administrative or clinical team
Completes required registration documentation
Provides identification and emergency contact details
2.2 Consent Discussion
The treating Doctor (or designated clinician) conducts a structured consent discussion covering:
Nature of treatment — Non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) under the FNON framework, which may include tDCS, TPS, taVNS, and CES
Off-label disclosure — TPS is CE-marked for Alzheimer's disease; its use for CP is investigational and off-label
Expected benefits — Potential improvement in motor symptoms, cognitive function, mood, pain, and fatigue (results vary; NIBS is adjunctive, not a replacement for medication)
Risks and side effects — Common (tingling, mild headache, skin redness), uncommon (burning sensation), rare (skin lesion, seizure)
Alternatives — Pharmacological treatment alone, DBS for eligible candidates, physiotherapy, other standard-of-care interventions
Right to withdraw — Participation is voluntary; withdrawal does not affect standard care
Data protection — How clinical data will be collected, stored, and used
2.3 Obtain Signatures
Patient signs the Informed Consent Form
Doctor co-signs confirming the consent discussion took place
Witness signature obtained (if required by local regulation)
WARNING: MANDATORY: No diagnostic or therapeutic procedures may be performed prior to informed consent being obtained. This includes the off-label disclosure for all investigational modalities used in Chronic Pain treatment. Non-compliance is a clinical governance violation.
Off-Label Disclosure Summary
Checklist
☐ Patient registration completed
☐ Consent discussion conducted by Doctor
☐ All modalities explained including off-label TPS status
☐ Patient questions addressed
☐ Consent form signed (patient + Doctor + witness)
☐ Signed consent filed in patient folder
Output
Signed informed consent. Patient cleared for assessment.
Forms Used
Informed Consent Form (Document 02 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 3: Psychological Intake & Psychometric Profiling
Purpose
This stage provides structured phenotyping and baseline quantification. The patient is evaluated by a clinical psychologist or trained clinical assistant who conducts a structured clinical interview and performs psychometric testing using the SOZO Patient Rating System (PRS). The data gathered here directly informs the phenotype identification in Stage 4 and the treatment plan in Stage 6.
Step-by-Step Procedure
3.1 Structured Clinical Interview
Conduct a systematic interview covering:
3.2 Digitise Medical Documentation
Scan or digitally archive all medical documents the patient has brought. Record what was provided in the Documentation Checklist section of the intake form.
3.3 SOZO PRS — Patient Rating System (Baseline)
The SOZO PRS is the standardised psychometric tool used to quantify symptom severity across clinically relevant domains. It establishes the baseline against which all future progress will be measured.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Select relevant domains based on diagnosis and presenting phenotype. Focus only on clinically meaningful domains — do not administer all scales to every patient.

Upon arrival at the clinic, the patient:
Is welcomed by the administrative or clinical team
Completes required registration documentation
Provides identification and emergency contact details
2.2 Consent Discussion
The treating Doctor (or designated clinician) conducts a structured consent discussion covering:
Nature of treatment — Non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) under the FNON framework, which may include tDCS, TPS, taVNS, and CES
Off-label disclosure — TPS is CE-marked for Alzheimer's disease; its use for MS is investigational and off-label
Expected benefits — Potential improvement in motor symptoms, cognitive function, mood, pain, and fatigue (results vary; NIBS is adjunctive, not a replacement for medication)
Risks and side effects — Common (tingling, mild headache, skin redness), uncommon (burning sensation), rare (skin lesion, seizure)
Alternatives — Pharmacological treatment alone, DBS for eligible candidates, physiotherapy, other standard-of-care interventions
Right to withdraw — Participation is voluntary; withdrawal does not affect standard care
Data protection — How clinical data will be collected, stored, and used
2.3 Obtain Signatures
Patient signs the Informed Consent Form
Doctor co-signs confirming the consent discussion took place
Witness signature obtained (if required by local regulation)
WARNING: MANDATORY: No diagnostic or therapeutic procedures may be performed prior to informed consent being obtained. This includes the off-label disclosure for all investigational modalities used in MS treatment. Non-compliance is a clinical governance violation.

☐ Patient registration completed
☐ Consent discussion conducted by Doctor
☐ All modalities explained including off-label TPS status
☐ Patient questions addressed

☐ Signed consent filed in patient folder
Output

Informed Consent Form (Document 02 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 3: Psychological Intake & Psychometric Profiling
Purpose
This stage provides structured phenotyping and baseline quantification. The patient is evaluated by a clinical psychologist or trained clinical assistant who conducts a structured clinical interview and performs psychometric testing using the SOZO Patient Rating System (PRS). The data gathered here directly informs the phenotype identification in Stage 4 and the treatment plan in Stage 6.
Step-by-Step Procedure
3.1 Structured Clinical Interview
Conduct a systematic interview covering:
3.2 Digitise Medical Documentation
Scan or digitally archive all medical documents the patient has brought. Record what was provided in the Documentation Checklist section of the intake form.
3.3 SOZO PRS — Patient Rating System (Baseline)
The SOZO PRS is the standardised psychometric tool used to quantify symptom severity across clinically relevant domains. It establishes the baseline against which all future progress will be measured.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Select relevant domains based on diagnosis and presenting phenotype. Focus only on clinically meaningful domains — do not administer all scales to every patient.
