About This Handbook
This handbook provides a complete step-by-step guide to the SOZO clinical pathway for Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI) using Functional Network-Oriented Neuromodulation (FNON). It is designed for daily clinical use by the treating Doctor and Clinical Assistants at SOZO Brain Center.
Each chapter corresponds to one stage of the patient journey, from initial appointment scheduling through active treatment and long-term therapy optimisation. The handbook integrates content from the SOZO FNON Clinical Protocol (Document A, v7.0), the FNON Master Assessment Document (v2.0), and the SOZO TBI Protocol Algorithm.
What is FNON?
Functional Network-Oriented Neuromodulation (FNON) is SOZO's proprietary clinical framework. Unlike conventional approaches that target isolated brain regions, FNON targets functional brain networks  the interconnected circuits that underlie TBI symptoms.
FNON MODEL: The FNON model follows a five-level clinical decision pathway from phenotyping through to iterative adjustment.
Available Modalities at SOZO
WARNING: Off-Label Disclosure: TPS (NEUROLITH®) use in Traumatic Brain Injury is INVESTIGATIONAL at SOZO Brain Center. It is not CE-marked or FDA-approved for TBI. All patients must provide written informed consent inclusive of the off-label TPS disclosure. TPS use is restricted to the prescribing Doctor only.
Roles and Responsibilities
WARNING: GOVERNANCE RULE: No Clinical Assistant may independently modify a treatment plan, change a montage, or adjust parameters without Doctor authorisation.
The Patient Journey at a Glance
Stage 1: Appointment Scheduling
Purpose
The patient schedules an appointment for an initial clinical evaluation and development of a preliminary neuromodulation plan. This first contact sets the tone for the entire clinical relationship and ensures that the patient arrives prepared for a comprehensive assessment.
Step-by-Step Procedure
1.1 Receive Enquiry
When a patient or referring physician contacts SOZO Brain Center, the administrative team collects initial information and determines appointment suitability.
1.2 Collect Preliminary Information
Full name, date of birth, and contact details
Referring physician details (if applicable)
Primary diagnosis (confirmed TBI or suspected)
Brief description of chief complaints
1.3 Pre-Appointment Instructions
Inform the patient to bring the following to their first visit:
All available medical records and discharge summaries
Brain imaging (MRI / CT scans)
Previous EEG reports (if any)
Neuropsychological test results
Complete and current medication list with dosages and timing
Laboratory results (recent blood work)
1.4 Confirm and Schedule
Confirm appointment date, time, and expected duration (allow 2–3 hours for initial assessment)
Send written confirmation via email with pre-appointment instructions
Send a reminder 24 hours before the appointment
Checklist
☐ Patient contact details recorded
☐ Referral information documented
☐ Pre-appointment instructions sent
☐ Appointment confirmed in scheduling system
☐ Reminder sent (24h before)
CLINICAL TIP: A well-prepared patient makes the assessment more efficient. Emphasise the importance of bringing a complete medication list with exact timing of each dose — medication state significantly affects assessment findings and later treatment planning.
Output
Initial visit scheduled. Patient informed of requirements.
Forms Used
Appointment Scheduling Form (Document 01 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 2: Informed Consent
Purpose
Before any diagnostic or therapeutic procedure is performed, the patient must provide written informed consent. This stage ensures the patient fully understands the nature, risks, benefits, and alternatives of the SOZO FNON neuromodulation programme, including the off-label status of TPS for Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
Step-by-Step Procedure
2.1 Welcome and Documentation
Upon arrival at the clinic, the patient:
Is welcomed by the administrative or clinical team
Completes required registration documentation
Provides identification and emergency contact details
2.2 Consent Discussion
The treating Doctor (or designated clinician) conducts a structured consent discussion covering:
Nature of treatment — Non-invasive brain stimulation (NIBS) under the FNON framework, which may include tDCS, TPS, taVNS, and CES
Off-label disclosure — TPS is CE-marked for Alzheimer's disease; its use for TBI is investigational and off-label
Expected benefits — Potential improvement in motor symptoms, cognitive function, mood, pain, and fatigue (results vary; NIBS is adjunctive, not a replacement for medication)
Risks and side effects — Common (tingling, mild headache, skin redness), uncommon (burning sensation), rare (skin lesion, seizure)
Alternatives — Pharmacological treatment alone, DBS for eligible candidates, physiotherapy, other standard-of-care interventions
Right to withdraw — Participation is voluntary; withdrawal does not affect standard care
Data protection — How clinical data will be collected, stored, and used
2.3 Obtain Signatures
Patient signs the Informed Consent Form
Doctor co-signs confirming the consent discussion took place
Witness signature obtained (if required by local regulation)
WARNING: MANDATORY: No diagnostic or therapeutic procedures may be performed prior to informed consent being obtained. This includes the off-label disclosure for all investigational modalities used in TBI treatment. Where cognitive capacity is reduced, an authorised representative must provide consent. Non-compliance is a clinical governance violation.
Off-Label Disclosure Summary
Checklist
☐ Patient registration completed
☐ Consent discussion conducted by Doctor
☐ All modalities explained including off-label TPS status
☐ Patient questions addressed
☐ Consent form signed (patient + Doctor + witness)
☐ Signed consent filed in patient folder
Output
Signed informed consent. Patient cleared for assessment.
Forms Used
Informed Consent Form (Document 02 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 3: Psychological Intake & Psychometric Profiling
Purpose
This stage provides structured phenotyping and baseline quantification. The patient is evaluated by a clinical psychologist or trained clinical assistant who conducts a structured clinical interview and performs psychometric testing using the SOZO Patient Rating System (PRS). The data gathered here directly informs the phenotype identification in Stage 4 and the treatment plan in Stage 6.
Step-by-Step Procedure
3.1 Structured Clinical Interview
Conduct a systematic interview covering:
3.2 Digitise Medical Documentation
Scan or digitally archive all medical documents the patient has brought. Record what was provided in the Documentation Checklist section of the intake form.
3.3 SOZO PRS — Patient Rating System (Baseline)
The SOZO PRS is the standardised psychometric tool used to quantify symptom severity across clinically relevant domains. It establishes the baseline against which all future progress will be measured.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Select relevant domains based on diagnosis and presenting phenotype. Focus only on clinically meaningful domains — do not administer all scales to every patient.

Stage 3: Psychological Intake & Psychometric Profiling
Purpose
This stage provides structured phenotyping and baseline quantification. Step-by-Step Procedure
3.1 Structured Clinical Interview
Conduct a systematic interview covering:
3.2 Digitise Medical Documentation
Scan or digitally archive all medical documents the patient has brought. Record what was provided in the Documentation Checklist section of the intake form.
3.3 SOZO PRS — Patient Rating System (Baseline)
The SOZO PRS is the standardised psychometric tool used to quantify symptom severity across clinically relevant domains. It establishes the baseline against which all future progress will be measured.
KEY PRINCIPLE: Select relevant domains based on diagnosis and presenting phenotype. Focus only on clinically meaningful domains — do not administer all scales to every patient.

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Inattention (difficulty sustaining focus on tasks)
Distractibility (ease of distraction by external stimuli)
Task completion (difficulty finishing started tasks)
Organisation (ability to manage tasks, materials, time)
Forgetfulness (forgetting daily tasks and appointments)
Hyperactivity (physical restlessness or motor overactivity)
Impulsivity (acting without thinking, interrupting)
Time management (chronic lateness, poor time sense)

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Worry frequency (how often uncontrollable worry occurs)
Worry controllability (ability to stop or redirect worry)
Restlessness / feeling on edge
Muscle tension (physical tension or somatic tension)
Irritability
Concentration difficulty
Fatigue (anxiety-driven exhaustion)
Panic / acute anxiety episodes

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Pain intensity (average over past week)
Pain frequency (proportion of waking hours in pain)
Pain interference with activities
Tenderness / allodynia
Stiffness (morning or movement-related)
Fatigue (pain-related exhaustion)
Physical function (ability to perform daily tasks)
Flare frequency and severity

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Depressed mood (persistent low mood, sadness)
Anhedonia (loss of interest or pleasure)
Guilt / worthlessness
Fatigue / energy loss
Psychomotor change (slowing or agitation)
Concentration / decision-making difficulty
Appetite / weight change
Suicidal ideation (passive; assess actively if present)

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Sleep onset latency (difficulty falling asleep)
Night-time awakenings (number and duration)
Early morning awakening (undesired early waking)
Overall sleep quality (restorative value)
Total sleep time (perceived hours)
Daytime sleepiness (Epworth-type rating)
Bedtime anxiety / pre-sleep arousal
Sleep effort / trying too hard to sleep

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Brain fog (cognitive cloudiness)
Fatigue (physical and mental)
Post-exertional malaise (worsening after activity)
Memory difficulties (working / episodic)
Concentration and sustained attention
Word-finding difficulties
Processing speed (mental sluggishness)
Headache / head pressure

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Fatigue (MS fatigue — distinct from sleepiness)
Walking ability (distance, speed, and safety)
Hand and arm coordination
Balance (standing and moving balance)
Visual clarity (blurring, double vision)
Spasticity (muscle stiffness and spasms)
Bladder / bowel function
Sensory symptoms (numbness, tingling, pain)

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Obsession frequency (how often obsessions occur)
Obsession distress (level of anxiety or distress caused)
Time spent on obsessions (hours per day)
Compulsion frequency (how often compulsions are performed)
Compulsion distress (distress if compulsions are resisted)
Resistance ability (capacity to resist compulsions)
Control over obsessions (ability to dismiss them)
Insight (recognition that obsessions/compulsions are excessive)

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Intrusive memories (unwanted, recurring trauma memories)
Flashbacks (reliving the trauma as if happening now)
Nightmares (trauma-related disturbing dreams)
Emotional reactivity (intense distress to triggers)
Avoidance of trauma-related situations
Avoidance of trauma-related thoughts / feelings
Hypervigilance (constantly on guard, scanning for danger)
Exaggerated startle response

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Headache (frequency and intensity)
Concentration (sustained focus difficulty)
Memory (short-term recall, working memory)
Processing speed (mental slowing)
Balance and dizziness
Fatigue (cognitive and physical)
Light sensitivity (photophobia)
Noise sensitivity (phonophobia)

handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.title: Primary Symptoms Domain — score each relevant item on a 0–10 scale (patient-rated):
handbook::primary_sympto_domain_score_ea.content:
Tinnitus loudness (perceived intensity)
Tinnitus distress (emotional impact)
Intrusiveness (interruption of daily activities)
Sound sensitivity (hyperacusis / misophonia)
Concentration impact
Severity in quiet environments
Perceived hearing difficulty
Effectiveness of masking / sound therapy

handbook::secondary_sympto_domain.title: Secondary Symptoms Domain:
handbook::secondary_sympto_domain.content:
Emotional dysregulation (anger, frustration bursts)
Frustration tolerance
Sleep onset / maintenance problems
Self-esteem and confidence
Interpersonal relationships
Motivation and initiation
Procrastination
Restlessness / internal hyperactivity
3.4 Preliminary Phenotype Impression
Based on the interview and PRS results, form a preliminary clinical impression:
Identify the dominant symptom cluster
Assign a preliminary phenotype: Inattentive, Hyperactive-Impulsive, Combined, Executive-Dominant, Emotional Dysregulation, Sluggish Cognitive Tempo (SCT), Working Memory, Reward-Deficit, or Comorbid Anxiety.
Record the patient's stated treatment goals
Note the most disabling symptom from the patient's perspective
Checklist
☐ Structured clinical interview completed
☐ All medical documentation digitised and archived
☐ SOZO PRS baseline scores recorded (relevant domains)
☐ Preliminary phenotype impression documented
☐ Patient treatment goals recorded
☐ Form signed by assessing clinician
Output
Baseline phenotype profile with quantified symptom severity. Data ready for medical examination (Stage 4).
Forms Used
Patient Intake & Medical History (Document 03) + Psychological Intake & PRS Baseline (Document 04)
Stage 4: Medical Examination
Purpose
The patient is examined by a SOZO doctor. This stage provides clinical validation of the phenotype identified in Stage 3, generates a network dysfunction hypothesis, and informs the preliminary selection of neuromodulation strategy. The assessment is based on the SOZO FNON Master Assessment Document (6-Network Bedside Assessment).
Step-by-Step Procedure
4.1 Six-Network Bedside Assessment (FNON Master Assessment)
This is the core clinical assessment tool. Each bedside test is scored as: HYPO (1) | NORMAL (0) | HYPER (2). The total assessment comprises 6 networks × 7–8 tests = 44 tests, with a maximum score of 88.
FNON ASSESSMENT: The 6-Network Bedside Assessment is the foundation of the FNON framework. It maps clinical observations to functional brain networks, enabling targeted neuromodulation.
THEORETICAL FOUNDATION: This assessment is grounded in the Triple Network Model (Menon, 2011), extended to incorporate Sensorimotor, Limbic, and Attention networks for a comprehensive 6-network clinical profile. The Salience Network (SN), anchored in the anterior insula and dACC, acts as a dynamic switch between DMN and CEN.
4.2 Extended Neurological Examination (Section C)
A structured neurological examination ensures anatomical correlation and exclusion of focal structural lesions:
Sensorimotor examination (tone, power, reflexes, coordination)
Pyramidal tract signs
Extrapyramidal signs (rigidity, bradykinesia, tremor assessment)
Frontal release signs
Cerebellar examination
Cranial nerves and visual-oculomotor assessment
Vestibular-oculomotor assessment
4.3 Phenotype Identification
Based on the combined 6-network assessment and neurological examination, the Doctor formally identifies the clinical phenotype. The FNON model recognises the following phenotypes in ADHD:
4.4 EEG Assessment (if available)
When technically available, EEG assessment provides additional neurophysiological data for network-oriented interpretation. The Doctor interprets EEG findings in the context of the 6-network assessment and phenotype identification.
4.5 Generate Network Hypothesis

handbook::secondary_sympto_domain.title: Secondary Symptoms Domain:
handbook::secondary_sympto_domain.content:
Depression / low mood
Anxiety
Apathy / motivation
Sleep quality
Agitation / restlessness
Wandering / disorientation
Appetite and weight
Independence in activities of daily living (ADL)
3.4 Preliminary Phenotype Impression
Based on the interview and PRS results, form a preliminary clinical impression:
Identify the dominant symptom cluster
Assign a preliminary phenotype: Amnestic MCI, Multi-Domain MCI, Early-Onset AD, Late-Onset AD, Posterior Cortical Atrophy, Logopenic, Behavioural/Frontal, Mixed AD-Vascular, or Apathy-Dominant.
Record the patient's stated treatment goals
Note the most disabling symptom from the patient's perspective
Checklist

☐ All medical documentation digitised and archived
☐ SOZO PRS baseline scores recorded (relevant domains)
☐ Preliminary phenotype impression documented
☐ Patient treatment goals recorded
☐ Form signed by assessing clinician
Output
Baseline phenotype profile with quantified symptom severity. Data ready for medical examination (Stage 4).

Patient Intake & Medical History (Document 03) + Psychological Intake & PRS Baseline (Document 04)

Social avoidance (due to tinnitus)
Work / occupational impact
Ability to relax
3.4 Preliminary Phenotype Impression
Based on the interview and PRS results, form a preliminary clinical impression:
Identify the dominant symptom cluster
Assign a preliminary phenotype: Tonal, Noise-Like, Pulsatile, Reactive, Somatic, Hyperacusis-Dominant, Depression Comorbid, Insomnia Comorbid, or Anxiety Comorbid.
Record the patient's stated treatment goals
Note the most disabling symptom from the patient's perspective
Checklist
☐ Structured clinical interview completed
☐ All medical documentation digitised and archived
☐ SOZO PRS baseline scores recorded (relevant domains)
☐ Preliminary phenotype impression documented
☐ Patient treatment goals recorded
☐ Form signed by assessing clinician
Output
Baseline phenotype profile with quantified symptom severity. Data ready for medical examination (Stage 4).
Forms Used
Patient Intake & Medical History (Document 03) + Psychological Intake & PRS Baseline (Document 04)
Stage 4: Medical Examination
Purpose
The patient is examined by a SOZO doctor. This stage provides clinical validation of the phenotype identified in Stage 3, generates a network dysfunction hypothesis, and informs the preliminary selection of neuromodulation strategy. The assessment is based on the SOZO FNON Master Assessment Document (6-Network Bedside Assessment).
Step-by-Step Procedure
4.1 Six-Network Bedside Assessment (FNON Master Assessment)
This is the core clinical assessment tool. Each bedside test is scored as: HYPO (1) | NORMAL (0) | HYPER (2). The total assessment comprises 6 networks × 7–8 tests = 44 tests, with a maximum score of 88.
FNON ASSESSMENT: The 6-Network Bedside Assessment is the foundation of the FNON framework. It maps clinical observations to functional brain networks, enabling targeted neuromodulation.
THEORETICAL FOUNDATION: This assessment is grounded in the Triple Network Model (Menon, 2011), extended to incorporate Sensorimotor, Limbic, and Attention networks for a comprehensive 6-network clinical profile. The Salience Network (SN), anchored in the anterior insula and dACC, acts as a dynamic switch between DMN and CEN.
4.2 Extended Neurological Examination (Section C)
A structured neurological examination ensures anatomical correlation and exclusion of focal structural lesions:
Sensorimotor examination (tone, power, reflexes, coordination)
Pyramidal tract signs
Extrapyramidal signs (rigidity, bradykinesia, tremor assessment)
Frontal release signs
Cerebellar examination
Cranial nerves and visual-oculomotor assessment
Vestibular-oculomotor assessment
4.3 Phenotype Identification
Based on the combined 6-network assessment and neurological examination, the Doctor formally identifies the clinical phenotype. The FNON model recognises the following phenotypes in TIN:
4.4 EEG Assessment (if available)
When technically available, EEG assessment provides additional neurophysiological data for network-oriented interpretation. The Doctor interprets EEG findings in the context of the 6-network assessment and phenotype identification.
4.5 Generate Network Hypothesis

The network hypothesis must address:
Which network(s) are primarily dysfunctional?
What is the hypothesised direction of dysfunction (hypo- vs. hyperactive)?
How does this map to the patient's symptom presentation?
What is the preliminary neuromodulation strategy?

handbook::tdcs_session_delivery.title: tDCS Session Delivery:
handbook::tdcs_session_delivery.content:
Apply electrodes per prescribed montage using MindCap system
Hydrate sponges with saline solution
Set parameters as prescribed (intensity, duration, ramp time)
Engage patient in prescribed concurrent task during stimulation
Monitor for adverse effects throughout

Monitor for adverse effects (headache, jaw discomfort, nausea)

handbook::tavns_session_delivery.title: taVNS Session Delivery:
handbook::tavns_session_delivery.content:
Place ear clip on prescribed ear (tragus / cymba conchae)
Adjust intensity to comfortable tingling without pain
Monitor for dizziness or syncope
7.3 Session Documentation
After each session, document in the Session Log:
Date, time, session number
Modality, montage, and parameters used
Medication state (stimulant medication adherence, timing of last dose, on-medication or drug holiday status)
Concurrent task performed
Any adverse events (graded 1–3)
Clinical observations and patient-reported effects
7.4 Adverse Event Management
WARNING: ADVERSE EVENT GRADING — Grade 1 (Mild): Tingling, mild headache, transient redness → CONTINUE, document. Grade 2 (Moderate): Persistent headache >4h, burning, mood change >24h → PAUSE, Doctor review within 24h. Grade 3 (Severe): Seizure, syncope, burn, neuro deficit, chest pain → STOP IMMEDIATELY, emergency response, no resumption without Doctor clearance.
7.5 Patient Training for Home-Based Therapy
During the in-clinic phase, train the patient (and caregiver if applicable) in:
Correct electrode placement for their prescribed montage
Sponge preparation and hydration
Device operation (power on/off, understanding Home Controlled Mode)
Skin inspection before and after each session
Stop criteria: burning, skin lesion, severe headache, dizziness, seizure → contact clinic immediately
Device cleaning and storage
7.6 Issue Home Protocol Prescription
When the patient transitions to home-based therapy, the Doctor issues a written prescription covering:
Prescribed device(s) and specific montage configuration
Stimulation parameters (intensity, duration, frequency)
Session schedule (daily or split sessions, maximum daily exposure)
Concurrent task programme (cognitive training, exercises, relaxation)
HOME USE SAFETY: For HDCkit tDCS, all settings are pre-programmed by the Doctor via HDCprog — the patient cannot modify parameters. Home use applies to tDCS, taVNS, and CES only. TPS is clinic-only.
Safety Limits Quick Reference
In-Clinic Task Pairing (by Montage)
CONCURRENT TASK PAIRING: Activity-dependent plasticity is critical to FNON outcomes. The concurrent task should match the network being stimulated.
Checklist (Per Session)
☐ Pre-session checks complete (skin, impedance, medication timing)
☐ Treatment delivered per prescribed protocol
☐ Concurrent task completed
☐ Post-session skin inspection
☐ Session documented in Session Log
☐ Any AEs documented and graded
Output
Active neuromodulation delivered. Patient trained for home continuation. Session data collected for outcome evaluation.
Forms Used
Session Log & AE Tracker (Document 08) + Device Training & Home Protocol (Document 09)
Stage 8: Follow-Up Evaluation (2–3 Months)
Purpose
After 2–3 months of treatment, the patient undergoes a dynamic reassessment, fully repeating the psychological assessment, medical examination, psychometric retesting, and EEG (if applicable). This ensures objective longitudinal comparison and drives evidence-based decisions about treatment continuation or modification.
Step-by-Step Procedure
8.1 Repeat Psychological Assessment (Stage 3)
Conduct a structured clinical interview to assess any changes in complaints, mood, function, and medication
Re-administer the SOZO PRS across all baseline domains
Record follow-up scores alongside baseline for direct comparison
8.2 Repeat Medical Examination (Stage 4)
Repeat the full 6-Network Bedside Assessment
Repeat the neurological examination
Perform EEG (if applicable and available)
Document all findings alongside baseline values
8.3 Compare Baseline vs. Follow-Up
For each domain, compare baseline and follow-up scores and assess whether changes are clinically meaningful:
WARNING: AVOIDING FALSE CLASSIFICATION: ADHD symptom expression varies significantly between on-medication and off-medication states. Standardise assessment conditions between baseline and follow-up — always assess in the same medication state (on or off). Document concurrent psychotherapy, coaching, or school/workplace accommodations when interpreting SOZO PRS changes.
8.4 Classify Response
8.5 Make Clinical Decision
☐ CONTINUE current protocol (patient is responding)

☐ RETURN to FNON Level 1 (non-responder → full reassessment)

☐ DISCONTINUE neuromodulation programme

Follow-Up Assessment (Document 10 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 9: Therapy Optimisation
Purpose
Based on the follow-up findings from Stage 8, the treatment is optimised using the FNON Level 4–5 adjustment pathway. This stage represents the iterative reassessment loop that ensures treatment remains aligned with the patient's evolving clinical profile.

9.1 Analyse Treatment Response (FNON Level 4)
FNON LEVEL 4: Response Evaluation — Systematically review all outcome data to determine whether the current protocol is achieving its intended network-level effects.
Review which domains improved, which did not, and which worsened
Review adverse event history from the treatment block
Assess medication timing variability and its potential impact on results
Review compliance data and home-use adherence
9.2 Reassess Phenotype (Return to FNON Level 1)
For non-responders or partial responders, formally reassess:
Has the dominant phenotype changed since baseline?
Are there new symptoms or symptom clusters?
Does the original network hypothesis still hold?
9.3 Revise Network Hypothesis (FNON Level 2)
FNON LEVEL 2 REVISION: Update the network hypothesis based on new clinical data. The FNON framework is designed to be iterative — hypotheses are refined, not abandoned.
Update the network hypothesis based on new clinical data
Consider alternative or additional network targets
Review EEG findings if new data is available
9.4 Modify Protocol (FNON Level 3 Adjustment)
The Doctor may make the following modifications:
Change tDCS montage (different electrode positions)
Adjust stimulation parameters (within safety limits)
Change TPS targets or pulse allocation
Add or remove modalities (e.g., add taVNS, adjust CES)
Change the S-O-Z-O sequencing
Adjust session frequency or block duration
Switch devices (HDCkit ↔ PlatoScience)
9.5 Non-Responder Escalation Pathway
FNON LEVEL 5: Adjustment — If no meaningful improvement after 8–10 sessions, follow the structured escalation pathway before considering discontinuation.
If no meaningful improvement after 8–10 sessions, follow this escalation:
Re-verify phenotype through clinical re-examination
Revisit network hypothesis (consider alternative networks)
Change montage AND/OR switch modality
Review medication timing (ON/OFF state variability)
Consider discontinuation if no response after 2 full blocks
WARNING: SAFETY RULE: DO NOT escalate intensity beyond safety limits. The protocol explicitly requires returning to FNON Level 1 for systematic reassessment rather than simple dose escalation.

Document all modifications with rationale
Issue updated home protocol prescription (if applicable)
Set the next review date
Continue therapy for another 2–3 months under the updated protocol

This cycle continues until treatment goals are met or a decision to discontinue is made.

handbook::tavns_session_delivery.title: taVNS Session Delivery:
handbook::tavns_session_delivery.content:
Place ear clip on prescribed ear (tragus / cymba conchae)
Adjust intensity to comfortable tingling without pain
Monitor for dizziness or syncope
7.3 Session Documentation
After each session, document in the Session Log:
Date, time, session number
Modality, montage, and parameters used
Medication state (cholinesterase inhibitor / memantine adherence, timing of last dose, caregiver report of behaviour today)
Concurrent task performed
Any adverse events (graded 1–3)
Clinical observations and patient-reported effects
7.4 Adverse Event Management
WARNING: ADVERSE EVENT GRADING — Grade 1 (Mild): Tingling, mild headache, transient redness → CONTINUE, document. Grade 2 (Moderate): Persistent headache >4h, burning, mood change >24h → PAUSE, Doctor review within 24h. Grade 3 (Severe): Seizure, syncope, burn, neuro deficit, chest pain → STOP IMMEDIATELY, emergency response, no resumption without Doctor clearance.
7.5 Patient Training for Home-Based Therapy
During the in-clinic phase, train the patient (and caregiver if applicable) in:
Correct electrode placement for their prescribed montage
Sponge preparation and hydration
Device operation (power on/off, understanding Home Controlled Mode)
Skin inspection before and after each session
Stop criteria: burning, skin lesion, severe headache, dizziness, seizure → contact clinic immediately
Device cleaning and storage
7.6 Issue Home Protocol Prescription
When the patient transitions to home-based therapy, the Doctor issues a written prescription covering:
Prescribed device(s) and specific montage configuration
Stimulation parameters (intensity, duration, frequency)
Session schedule (daily or split sessions, maximum daily exposure)
Concurrent task programme (cognitive training, exercises, relaxation)
HOME USE SAFETY: For HDCkit tDCS, all settings are pre-programmed by the Doctor via HDCprog — the patient cannot modify parameters. Home use applies to tDCS, taVNS, and CES only. TPS is clinic-only.

In-Clinic Task Pairing (by Montage)
CONCURRENT TASK PAIRING: Activity-dependent plasticity is critical to FNON outcomes. The concurrent task should match the network being stimulated.

☐ Pre-session checks complete (skin, impedance, medication timing)

☐ Concurrent task completed
☐ Post-session skin inspection
☐ Session documented in Session Log

WARNING: AVOIDING FALSE CLASSIFICATION: Cognitive performance in AD/MCI fluctuates with fatigue, infection, dehydration, and medication state. Adjust intensity to comfortable tingling without pain
Monitor for dizziness or syncope
7.3 Session Documentation

Date, time, session number
Modality, montage, and parameters used
Medication state (antidepressant adherence, timing of last dose, any recent medication changes)
Concurrent task performed
Any adverse events (graded 1–3)
Clinical observations and patient-reported effects
7.4 Adverse Event Management
WARNING: ADVERSE EVENT GRADING — Grade 1 (Mild): Tingling, mild headache, transient redness → CONTINUE, document. Grade 2 (Moderate): Persistent headache >4h, burning, mood change >24h → PAUSE, Doctor review within 24h. Grade 3 (Severe): Seizure, syncope, burn, neuro deficit, chest pain → STOP IMMEDIATELY, emergency response, no resumption without Doctor clearance.
7.5 Patient Training for Home-Based Therapy
During the in-clinic phase, train the patient (and caregiver if applicable) in:
Correct electrode placement for their prescribed montage
Sponge preparation and hydration
Device operation (power on/off, understanding Home Controlled Mode)
Skin inspection before and after each session
Stop criteria: burning, skin lesion, severe headache, dizziness, seizure → contact clinic immediately
Device cleaning and storage
7.6 Issue Home Protocol Prescription
When the patient transitions to home-based therapy, the Doctor issues a written prescription covering:
Prescribed device(s) and specific montage configuration
Stimulation parameters (intensity, duration, frequency)
Session schedule (daily or split sessions, maximum daily exposure)
Concurrent task programme (cognitive training, exercises, relaxation)
HOME USE SAFETY: For HDCkit tDCS, all settings are pre-programmed by the Doctor via HDCprog — the patient cannot modify parameters. Home use applies to tDCS, taVNS, and CES only. TPS is clinic-only.

After each optimisation (Stage 9), the patient returns to active treatment (Stage 7) under the updated protocol, followed by reassessment (Stage 8), and further optimisation (Stage 9) as needed. This cycle continues until treatment goals are met or a decision to discontinue is made.

Updated treatment protocol. Revised network hypothesis. Patient continues under the optimised plan.

handbook::tavns_session_delivery.title: taVNS Session Delivery:
handbook::tavns_session_delivery.content:
Place ear clip on prescribed ear (tragus / cymba conchae)
Adjust intensity to comfortable tingling without pain
Monitor for dizziness or syncope
7.3 Session Documentation
After each session, document in the Session Log:
Date, time, session number
Modality, montage, and parameters used
Medication state (SSRI/clomipramine adherence, timing of last dose, OCD symptom severity rating at session start)
Concurrent task performed
Any adverse events (graded 1–3)
Clinical observations and patient-reported effects
7.4 Adverse Event Management
WARNING: ADVERSE EVENT GRADING — Grade 1 (Mild): Tingling, mild headache, transient redness → CONTINUE, document. Grade 2 (Moderate): Persistent headache >4h, burning, mood change >24h → PAUSE, Doctor review within 24h. Grade 3 (Severe): Seizure, syncope, burn, neuro deficit, chest pain → STOP IMMEDIATELY, emergency response, no resumption without Doctor clearance.
7.5 Patient Training for Home-Based Therapy
During the in-clinic phase, train the patient (and caregiver if applicable) in:
Correct electrode placement for their prescribed montage
Sponge preparation and hydration
Device operation (power on/off, understanding Home Controlled Mode)
Skin inspection before and after each session
Stop criteria: burning, skin lesion, severe headache, dizziness, seizure → contact clinic immediately
Device cleaning and storage
7.6 Issue Home Protocol Prescription
When the patient transitions to home-based therapy, the Doctor issues a written prescription covering:
Prescribed device(s) and specific montage configuration
Stimulation parameters (intensity, duration, frequency)
Session schedule (daily or split sessions, maximum daily exposure)
Concurrent task programme (cognitive training, exercises, relaxation)
HOME USE SAFETY: For HDCkit tDCS, all settings are pre-programmed by the Doctor via HDCprog — the patient cannot modify parameters. Home use applies to tDCS, taVNS, and CES only. TPS is clinic-only.
Safety Limits Quick Reference
In-Clinic Task Pairing (by Montage)
CONCURRENT TASK PAIRING: Activity-dependent plasticity is critical to FNON outcomes. The concurrent task should match the network being stimulated.
Checklist (Per Session)
☐ Pre-session checks complete (skin, impedance, medication timing)
☐ Treatment delivered per prescribed protocol
☐ Concurrent task completed
☐ Post-session skin inspection
☐ Session documented in Session Log
☐ Any AEs documented and graded
Output
Active neuromodulation delivered. Patient trained for home continuation. Session data collected for outcome evaluation.
Forms Used
Session Log & AE Tracker (Document 08) + Device Training & Home Protocol (Document 09)
Stage 8: Follow-Up Evaluation (2–3 Months)
Purpose
After 2–3 months of treatment, the patient undergoes a dynamic reassessment, fully repeating the psychological assessment, medical examination, psychometric retesting, and EEG (if applicable). This ensures objective longitudinal comparison and drives evidence-based decisions about treatment continuation or modification.
Step-by-Step Procedure
8.1 Repeat Psychological Assessment (Stage 3)
Conduct a structured clinical interview to assess any changes in complaints, mood, function, and medication
Re-administer the SOZO PRS across all baseline domains
Record follow-up scores alongside baseline for direct comparison
8.2 Repeat Medical Examination (Stage 4)
Repeat the full 6-Network Bedside Assessment
Repeat the neurological examination
Perform EEG (if applicable and available)
Document all findings alongside baseline values
8.3 Compare Baseline vs. Follow-Up
For each domain, compare baseline and follow-up scores and assess whether changes are clinically meaningful:
WARNING: AVOIDING FALSE CLASSIFICATION: OCD symptoms fluctuate with stress, life changes, and the natural waxing/waning course of the disorder. Standardise assessment conditions between baseline and follow-up. Document concurrent ERP (Exposure and Response Prevention) therapy milestones, medication changes, and significant life stressors when interpreting SOZO PRS changes. OCD insight variability must be considered when comparing self-rated scores.
8.4 Classify Response
8.5 Make Clinical Decision
☐ CONTINUE current protocol (patient is responding)
☐ MODIFY protocol (partial response → proceed to Stage 9)
☐ RETURN to FNON Level 1 (non-responder → full reassessment)
☐ TRANSITION to home-only maintenance
☐ DISCONTINUE neuromodulation programme
Output
Objective outcome comparison (baseline vs. follow-up). Response classification. Evidence-based clinical decision.
Forms Used
Follow-Up Assessment (Document 10 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 9: Therapy Optimisation
Purpose
Based on the follow-up findings from Stage 8, the treatment is optimised using the FNON Level 4–5 adjustment pathway. This stage represents the iterative reassessment loop that ensures treatment remains aligned with the patient's evolving clinical profile.
Step-by-Step Procedure
9.1 Analyse Treatment Response (FNON Level 4)
FNON LEVEL 4: Response Evaluation — Systematically review all outcome data to determine whether the current protocol is achieving its intended network-level effects.
Review which domains improved, which did not, and which worsened
Review adverse event history from the treatment block
Assess medication timing variability and its potential impact on results
Review compliance data and home-use adherence
9.2 Reassess Phenotype (Return to FNON Level 1)
For non-responders or partial responders, formally reassess:
Has the dominant phenotype changed since baseline?
Are there new symptoms or symptom clusters?
Does the original network hypothesis still hold?
9.3 Revise Network Hypothesis (FNON Level 2)
FNON LEVEL 2 REVISION: Update the network hypothesis based on new clinical data. The FNON framework is designed to be iterative — hypotheses are refined, not abandoned.
Update the network hypothesis based on new clinical data
Consider alternative or additional network targets
Review EEG findings if new data is available
9.4 Modify Protocol (FNON Level 3 Adjustment)
The Doctor may make the following modifications:
Change tDCS montage (different electrode positions)
Adjust stimulation parameters (within safety limits)
Change TPS targets or pulse allocation
Add or remove modalities (e.g., add taVNS, adjust CES)
Change the S-O-Z-O sequencing
Adjust session frequency or block duration
Switch devices (HDCkit ↔ PlatoScience)
9.5 Non-Responder Escalation Pathway
FNON LEVEL 5: Adjustment — If no meaningful improvement after 8–10 sessions, follow the structured escalation pathway before considering discontinuation.
If no meaningful improvement after 8–10 sessions, follow this escalation:
Re-verify phenotype through clinical re-examination
Revisit network hypothesis (consider alternative networks)
Change montage AND/OR switch modality
Review medication timing (ON/OFF state variability)
Consider discontinuation if no response after 2 full blocks
WARNING: SAFETY RULE: DO NOT escalate intensity beyond safety limits. The protocol explicitly requires returning to FNON Level 1 for systematic reassessment rather than simple dose escalation.
9.6 Issue Updated Treatment Plan
Document all modifications with rationale
Issue updated home protocol prescription (if applicable)
Set the next review date
Continue therapy for another 2–3 months under the updated protocol
The Continuous Improvement Cycle
THE FNON ITERATION LOOP: The FNON framework is designed to be iterative: Stages 7 → 8 → 9 form a continuous cycle. After each optimisation (Stage 9), the patient returns to active treatment (Stage 7) under the updated protocol, followed by reassessment (Stage 8), and further optimisation (Stage 9) as needed. This cycle continues until treatment goals are met or a decision to discontinue is made.
Output
Updated treatment protocol. Revised network hypothesis. Patient continues under the optimised plan.
Forms Used
Therapy Optimisation Record (Document 11 in the Patient Folder)
Appendices
Appendix A: Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria

WARNING: ADVERSE EVENT GRADING — Grade 1 (Mild): Tingling, mild headache, transient redness → CONTINUE, document. Grade 2 (Moderate): Persistent headache >4h, burning, mood change >24h → PAUSE, Doctor review within 24h. Grade 3 (Severe): Seizure, syncope, burn, neuro deficit, chest pain → STOP IMMEDIATELY, emergency response, no resumption without Doctor clearance.
7.5 Patient Training for Home-Based Therapy
During the in-clinic phase, train the patient (and caregiver if applicable) in:
Correct electrode placement for their prescribed montage
Sponge preparation and hydration
Device operation (power on/off, understanding Home Controlled Mode)
Skin inspection before and after each session
Stop criteria: burning, skin lesion, severe headache, dizziness, seizure → contact clinic immediately
Device cleaning and storage
7.6 Issue Home Protocol Prescription
When the patient transitions to home-based therapy, the Doctor issues a written prescription covering:
Prescribed device(s) and specific montage configuration
Stimulation parameters (intensity, duration, frequency)
Session schedule (daily or split sessions, maximum daily exposure)
Concurrent task programme (cognitive training, exercises, relaxation)
HOME USE SAFETY: For HDCkit tDCS, all settings are pre-programmed by the Doctor via HDCprog — the patient cannot modify parameters. Home use applies to tDCS, taVNS, and CES only. TPS is clinic-only.
Safety Limits Quick Reference
In-Clinic Task Pairing (by Montage)
CONCURRENT TASK PAIRING: Activity-dependent plasticity is critical to FNON outcomes. The concurrent task should match the network being stimulated.
Checklist (Per Session)
☐ Pre-session checks complete (skin, impedance, medication timing)
☐ Treatment delivered per prescribed protocol
☐ Concurrent task completed
☐ Post-session skin inspection
☐ Session documented in Session Log
☐ Any AEs documented and graded
Output
Active neuromodulation delivered. Patient trained for home continuation. Session data collected for outcome evaluation.
Forms Used
Session Log & AE Tracker (Document 08) + Device Training & Home Protocol (Document 09)
Stage 8: Follow-Up Evaluation (2–3 Months)
Purpose
After 2–3 months of treatment, the patient undergoes a dynamic reassessment, fully repeating the psychological assessment, medical examination, psychometric retesting, and EEG (if applicable). This ensures objective longitudinal comparison and drives evidence-based decisions about treatment continuation or modification.
Step-by-Step Procedure
8.1 Repeat Psychological Assessment (Stage 3)
Conduct a structured clinical interview to assess any changes in complaints, mood, function, and medication
Re-administer the SOZO PRS across all baseline domains
Record follow-up scores alongside baseline for direct comparison
8.2 Repeat Medical Examination (Stage 4)
Repeat the full 6-Network Bedside Assessment
Repeat the neurological examination
Perform EEG (if applicable and available)
Document all findings alongside baseline values
8.3 Compare Baseline vs. Follow-Up
For each domain, compare baseline and follow-up scores and assess whether changes are clinically meaningful:
WARNING: AVOIDING FALSE CLASSIFICATION: TBI symptom recovery is non-linear and confounded by spontaneous recovery, sleep quality, and psychological factors. Standardise assessment conditions between baseline and follow-up. Acute illness, stress events, or medication changes in the preceding two weeks must be documented. Distinguish NIBS-attributable change from ongoing spontaneous neurological recovery, particularly in the first 12 months post-injury.
8.4 Classify Response
8.5 Make Clinical Decision
☐ CONTINUE current protocol (patient is responding)
☐ MODIFY protocol (partial response → proceed to Stage 9)
☐ RETURN to FNON Level 1 (non-responder → full reassessment)
☐ TRANSITION to home-only maintenance
☐ DISCONTINUE neuromodulation programme
Output
Objective outcome comparison (baseline vs. follow-up). Response classification. Evidence-based clinical decision.
Forms Used
Follow-Up Assessment (Document 10 in the Patient Folder)
Stage 9: Therapy Optimisation
Purpose
Based on the follow-up findings from Stage 8, the treatment is optimised using the FNON Level 4–5 adjustment pathway. This stage represents the iterative reassessment loop that ensures treatment remains aligned with the patient's evolving clinical profile.
Step-by-Step Procedure
9.1 Analyse Treatment Response (FNON Level 4)
FNON LEVEL 4: Response Evaluation — Systematically review all outcome data to determine whether the current protocol is achieving its intended network-level effects.
Review which domains improved, which did not, and which worsened
Review adverse event history from the treatment block
Assess medication timing variability and its potential impact on results
Review compliance data and home-use adherence
9.2 Reassess Phenotype (Return to FNON Level 1)
For non-responders or partial responders, formally reassess:
Has the dominant phenotype changed since baseline?
Are there new symptoms or symptom clusters?
Does the original network hypothesis still hold?
9.3 Revise Network Hypothesis (FNON Level 2)
FNON LEVEL 2 REVISION: Update the network hypothesis based on new clinical data. The FNON framework is designed to be iterative — hypotheses are refined, not abandoned.
Update the network hypothesis based on new clinical data
Consider alternative or additional network targets
Review EEG findings if new data is available
9.4 Modify Protocol (FNON Level 3 Adjustment)
The Doctor may make the following modifications:
Change tDCS montage (different electrode positions)
Adjust stimulation parameters (within safety limits)
Change TPS targets or pulse allocation
Add or remove modalities (e.g., add taVNS, adjust CES)
Change the S-O-Z-O sequencing
Adjust session frequency or block duration
Switch devices (HDCkit ↔ PlatoScience)
9.5 Non-Responder Escalation Pathway
FNON LEVEL 5: Adjustment — If no meaningful improvement after 8–10 sessions, follow the structured escalation pathway before considering discontinuation.
If no meaningful improvement after 8–10 sessions, follow this escalation:
Re-verify phenotype through clinical re-examination
Revisit network hypothesis (consider alternative networks)
Change montage AND/OR switch modality
Review medication timing (ON/OFF state variability)
Consider discontinuation if no response after 2 full blocks
WARNING: SAFETY RULE: DO NOT escalate intensity beyond safety limits. The protocol explicitly requires returning to FNON Level 1 for systematic reassessment rather than simple dose escalation.
9.6 Issue Updated Treatment Plan
Document all modifications with rationale
Issue updated home protocol prescription (if applicable)
Set the next review date
Continue therapy for another 2–3 months under the updated protocol
The Continuous Improvement Cycle
THE FNON ITERATION LOOP: The FNON framework is designed to be iterative: Stages 7 → 8 → 9 form a continuous cycle. After each optimisation (Stage 9), the patient returns to active treatment (Stage 7) under the updated protocol, followed by reassessment (Stage 8), and further optimisation (Stage 9) as needed. This cycle continues until treatment goals are met or a decision to discontinue is made.
Output
Updated treatment protocol. Revised network hypothesis. Patient continues under the optimised plan.
Forms Used
Therapy Optimisation Record (Document 11 in the Patient Folder)
Appendices
Appendix A: Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria

handbook::tavns_session_delivery.title: taVNS Session Delivery:
handbook::tavns_session_delivery.content:
Place ear clip on prescribed ear (tragus / cymba conchae)
Adjust intensity to comfortable tingling without pain
Monitor for dizziness or syncope

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed diagnosis of ADHD (DSM-5 or ICD-11 criteria; clinical or formal neuropsychological assessment)
Moderate to severe symptom impairment in at least two settings (work, school, home)
Age 16+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and ADHD outcome measures (ASRS or equivalent) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease or MCI (NIA-AA criteria or equivalent; specialist-confirmed)
MMSE ≥15 or MoCA ≥10 at baseline (sufficient for meaningful participation)
Age 50+ years (early-onset AD: 40+ with specialist confirmation)
Able to attend sessions with caregiver support or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (patient with capacity, or guardian/representative for reduced capacity)
Baseline SOZO PRS and cognitive outcome measures (MoCA or MMSE) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed diagnosis of Generalized Anxiety Disorder (ICD-10 F41.1 or DSM-5 criteria)
Moderate to severe anxiety (GAD-7 ≥10 or equivalent; functionally impairing)
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and anxiety outcome measures (GAD-7 or equivalent) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed ASD diagnosis (DSM-5 or ICD-11 criteria; clinical or formal assessment)
Age 12+ years (or adult); parental/guardian consent for minors
Able to tolerate device placement with adequate behavioural support
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol with caregiver support
Written informed consent (patient/guardian; including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and functional outcome measures completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed chronic pain condition (≥3 months duration; specialist-diagnosed or GP-referred with documented history)
Moderate to severe pain interference with function (NRS ≥5 average, or PRS ≥5)
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and pain outcome measures (NRS + BPI or equivalent) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed diagnosis of Major Depressive Disorder (ICD-10 F32/F33 or DSM-5 criteria)
Moderate to severe depressive episode or treatment-resistant course (PHQ-9 ≥10 or equivalent)
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and depression outcome measures (PHQ-9 or equivalent) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed chronic insomnia disorder (DSM-5 / ICSD-3; symptoms ≥3 months, ≥3 nights/week)
Moderate or severe insomnia severity (ISI ≥15 or PRS primary score ≥5 average)
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and insomnia outcome measures (ISI or equivalent) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed Long COVID / Post-COVID Syndrome (WHO 2021 definition; symptoms ≥12 weeks post-infection)
Cognitive or fatigue symptoms causing functional impairment
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and cognitive outcome measures completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed Multiple Sclerosis diagnosis (McDonald criteria 2017 or equivalent; neurologist-confirmed)
Clinically stable MS (no relapse within 3 months; EDSS ≤7.0 for active participation)
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol (with caregiver if needed)
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and MS outcome measures (MFIS, EDSS, MoCA) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed OCD diagnosis (DSM-5 / ICD-11 criteria; Y-BOCS ≥16 or equivalent)
Moderate to severe OCD burden causing significant functional impairment
Age 16+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and OCD outcome measures (Y-BOCS or OCI-R) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed PTSD diagnosis (DSM-5 / ICD-11 criteria; clinician-administered CAPS-5 or PCL-5 ≥33 preferred)
Moderate to severe PTSD symptom burden causing functional impairment
Age 18+ years; sufficient stabilisation for NIBS (not in acute crisis)
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure; trauma-sensitive process)
Baseline SOZO PRS and PTSD outcome measures (PCL-5 or equivalent) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed ischaemic or haemorrhagic stroke (CT/MRI confirmed; specialist-diagnosed)
Chronic stroke phase (≥3 months post-event; beyond peak spontaneous recovery window) or subacute with specialist approval
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol (with caregiver if needed)
Written informed consent (patient with capacity, or guardian/representative for reduced capacity)
Baseline SOZO PRS and functional outcome measures (Barthel or MAS) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed ischaemic or haemorrhagic stroke (CT/MRI confirmed; specialist-diagnosed)
Chronic stroke phase (≥3 months post-event; beyond peak spontaneous recovery window) or subacute with specialist approval
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol (with caregiver if needed)
Written informed consent (patient with capacity, or guardian/representative for reduced capacity)
Baseline SOZO PRS and functional outcome measures (Barthel or MAS) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed TBI diagnosis (mild, moderate, or severe; specialist-confirmed with CT/MRI or clinical criteria)
Persistent post-TBI symptoms causing functional impairment (>3 months from injury for mild TBI)
Age 16+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and TBI outcome measures (PCSS or equivalent) completed

handbook::inclusion_criteria.title: Inclusion Criteria:
handbook::inclusion_criteria.content:
Confirmed chronic tinnitus (duration ≥3 months; audiological assessment completed)
Tinnitus causing functional distress or impairment (THI moderate or above, or PRS score ≥4)
Age 18+ years
Able to attend sessions or comply with home-use protocol
Written informed consent (including TPS off-label disclosure)
Baseline SOZO PRS and tinnitus outcome measures (THI or equivalent) completed

handbook::absolute_exclusion_criteria.content:
Intracranial metallic hardware in stimulation path
Cochlear implant
Skull defects, craniectomy, or recent craniotomy
Active intracranial tumour
Open wounds at electrode sites
Pregnancy (tDCS, TPS)
Inability to provide informed consent

handbook::conditions_requiring_discussio.title: Conditions Requiring Discussion (NOT absolute — require documented risk–benefit assessment):
handbook::conditions_requiring_discussio.content:
Cardiac pacemaker or defibrillator
Epilepsy or seizure history
Unstable comorbid bipolar disorder or psychosis
Active substance abuse (stimulant misuse requiring specialist management first)
Severe tic disorder (Tourette's) requiring specialist review
Coagulation disorders or anticoagulants (especially TPS)
Dermatological conditions at electrode sites
Uncontrolled hypertension (stimulant medication interaction — Doctor review required)

Anxiety-tinnitus comorbidity, insomnia, hyperacusis, depression-comorbid phenotype
20–60 min daily, Alpha-Stim AID, especially DE, HY, RE phenotypes
Wk 1–4: L-A1 cathodal + L-DLPFC anodal tDCS (5×/wk) → Wk 2–6: TPS A1/DLPFC
Wk 1–4: S1/DLPFC tDCS → Wk 3–6: TPS somatosensory/A1
Wk 1–2: CES stabilization → Wk 2–6: tDCS bilateral A1 cathodal + TPS A1/SN
Sequential: TPS (highest burden) → Maintenance L-DLPFC tDCS + taVNS

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.title: 1. SOZO Operational Response Definition
responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to stimulant peak/trough variability and sleep-dependent cognitive performance.

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.title: 1. SOZO Operational Response Definition
responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to sundowning, time-of-day cognitive fluctuation, and caregiver vs patient reporting discordance.

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.title: 1. SOZO Operational Response Definition
responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to situational anxiety variation and pre-assessment anticipatory anxiety.

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to routine disruption effects on behaviour and informant reporting variance across settings.

Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to pain flare timing, rescue medication use, and day-to-day pain variability.

Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to night-to-night sleep variability and weekend vs weekday pattern differences.

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to post-exertional malaise and good day/bad day fluctuation characteristic of Long COVID.

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.title: 1. SOZO Operational Response Definition
responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to relapse-remission fluctuation and Uhthoff's phenomenon during heat exposure.

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to OCD symptom fluctuation during dose adjustment periods and stress-triggered OCD worsening.

responder_tracking::1_sozo_operational_r.content:
Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.
This avoids "false non-responder" labelling due to trauma trigger exposure before assessment and dissociation during testing.

Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.

Define response at end of block (typically 10–20 sessions). Patient must meet criteria in at least one domain.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed at stimulant trough and follow-up at peak (or vice versa), or if the night before differed in sleep quality, you can create a false responder or non-responder. Always document stimulant timing, time of day, and prior-night sleep at each assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed in the morning (peak cognitive window) and follow-up in the afternoon (sundowning period), or if caregiver vs patient reporting differs across assessments, you can create a false responder or non-responder. Always assess at the same time of day with the same informant.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed during a low-anxiety period and follow-up occurs before a stressful event (or vice versa), or if caffeine intake or sleep quality differs, you can create a false responder or non-responder. Document anxiety state, caffeine intake, and anticipatory triggers at each assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: Environmental sensory load differences between baseline and follow-up, routine disruption, or inconsistent informant (parent vs teacher vs self-report) can create false responders or non-responders. Always assess in the same environment, same time of day, with the same primary informant.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed on a low-pain day and follow-up during a pain flare (or vice versa), or if rescue medication was used differently before assessments, you can create a false responder or non-responder. Always document pain NRS, rescue medication use, activity level, and stress level at assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed during diurnal low-mood period and follow-up during a high-mood period (or vice versa), or if medication has not reached steady-state, you can create a false responder or non-responder. Always document time of day, sleep quality, and medication phase at each assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: Night-to-night sleep variability is inherent in insomnia. If baseline uses a single poor-sleep night and follow-up uses a single good-sleep night (or vice versa), you will create false responders or non-responders. Always use 7-day sleep diary averages for assessments; document weekend vs weekday patterns and sleep medication use.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: Post-exertional malaise (PEM) triggered by activity before assessment can create a false non-responder. Good day vs bad day fluctuation is extreme in Long COVID. Always assess on a rested day, document exertion level 24–48h prior, screen for viral reactivation episodes, and use consistent pacing.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed during remission and follow-up occurs during a relapse (or vice versa), or if heat exposure triggers Uhthoff's phenomenon before assessment, you can create a false responder or non-responder. Always document relapse/remission status, Uhthoff's exposure, and fatigue time-of-day at assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed during a low-stress period and follow-up occurs during a stress-triggered OCD worsening, or if SSRI dose has been recently adjusted, you can create a false non-responder. Always document current stress level, recent triggers, medication phase, and ERP therapy stage at each assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed on a low-symptom day and follow-up occurs after a trauma trigger exposure or a nightmare-disrupted night, you can create a false non-responder. Dissociation during testing invalidates scores. Always document trigger exposure, nightmare frequency, sleep quality, and dissociation state at each assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: Spontaneous neurological recovery in the first 3–6 months post-stroke can create false responders independent of tDCS. Fatigue-related performance decline during sessions can create false non-responders. Always document time since stroke, session fatigue level, and recovery phase at each assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: If baseline is assessed during a low-symptom day and follow-up occurs after physical or cognitive exertion (or vice versa), post-exertional symptom worsening can create a false non-responder. Always document exertion level, headache state, and cognitive fatigue at each assessment.

responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.title: 3C. Measurement Predictors
responder_tracking::3c_measurement_predi.content: WARNING: Noise exposure before assessment, high stress levels, sleep disruption, and time of day (tinnitus typically louder at night and in quiet environments) can create false responders or non-responders. Always assess at the same quiet-environment time of day with consistent pre-session noise exposure control.

responder_tracking::6_sozo_nonresponder_.content: If no meaningful improvement after 8–10 sessions, follow FNON Level 5 adjustment pathway: Reassess phenotype → Revise network hypothesis → Modify montage/modality.

responder_tracking::7_stimulantatomoxeti.title: 7. Stimulant/Atomoxetine-tDCS Scheduling & Documentation
responder_tracking::7_stimulantatomoxeti.content: tDCS effects are state-dependent. Dopaminergic/noradrenergic tone from stimulants modulates prefrontal excitability and NMDA-dependent plasticity.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE stimulant timing window for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. Assessing during stimulant peak vs trough is one of the most common sources of false classification in ADHD.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE time-of-day window for all assessments and keep it CONSISTENT. Time-of-day cognitive fluctuation in Alzheimer's/MCI is one of the most common sources of false classification.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE medication state for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. Assessing during peak anxiolytic effect vs trough is one of the most common sources of false classification.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Use the SAME informant, same environment, and same medication state for all assessments in a block. Multi-informant variance and environmental inconsistency are the most common sources of false classification in ASD.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE baseline pain state context for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. Pain flare timing and rescue medication use are the most common sources of false classification in chronic pain.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE medication phase for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. Assessing patients during SSRI wash-in vs steady-state is one of the most common sources of false classification.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Assess using 7-day sleep diary averages, NOT single-night measures. Use the SAME sleep medication timing and weekday/weekend pattern for all assessment windows to ensure valid comparison.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Assess ONLY on a rested day with no significant exertion in the preceding 24–48 hours. Post-exertional malaise is the most critical source of false classification in Long COVID.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Assess ONLY in clinically stable remission and keep this CONSISTENT across the block. Relapse vs remission state differences are the most critical source of false classification in MS.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE medication phase for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. OCD symptom fluctuation during SSRI dose titration and stress-triggered worsening are the most common sources of false classification.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE medication state and trigger-exposure context for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. Trigger exposure before assessment and SSRI wash-in variability are the most common sources of false classification in PTSD.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE medication and recovery phase state for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. Spontaneous recovery combined with treatment effects is one of the most common attribution challenges in stroke rehabilitation.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE medication timing window for a block and keep it CONSISTENT. Post-exertional symptom fluctuation and inconsistent medication timing are the most common sources of false classification in TBI.

responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.title: 7B. SOZO Scheduling Rules
responder_tracking::7b_sozo_scheduling_r.content: SOZO Standard Rule: Choose ONE pre-session noise environment and time-of-day window for all assessments. Noise exposure before assessment and time-of-day variability are the most common sources of false classification in tinnitus.
